title: Team Sky

infobox.value: [Servais Knaven](/wiki/Servais_Knaven "Servais Knaven")[[1]](#cite_note-sknp-1) [Brett Lancaster](/wiki/Brett_Lancaster "Brett Lancaster")[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Matteo Tosatto](/wiki/Matteo_Tosatto "Matteo Tosatto")

(*[UCI](/wiki/Union_Cycliste_Internationale "Union Cycliste Internationale") team code:* **IGD**[[3]](#cite_note-3)) (stylised as **INEOS Grenadiers**) (formerly **Team Sky** from 2010 to 2019, and **Team Ineos** from 2019 to 2020) is a British professional [cycling team](/wiki/Cycling_team "Cycling team") that competes at the [UCI WorldTeam](/wiki/UCI_WorldTeam "UCI WorldTeam") level. The team is based at the [National Cycling Centre](/wiki/Manchester_Velodrome "Manchester Velodrome") in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), England, with a logistics base in [Deinze](/wiki/Deinze "Deinze"), Belgium.[[4]](#cite_note-guardian.co.uk-4) The team is managed by [British Cycling](/wiki/British_Cycling "British Cycling")'s former performance director, [Sir Dave Brailsford](/wiki/Dave_Brailsford "Dave Brailsford"). The company **Tour Racing Ltd.**[[5]](#cite_note-5) is the corporate entity behind the team in all its iterations, which in line with cycling practice adopts the name of their current primary sponsor.

**Ineos Grenadiers** (*[UCI](/wiki/Union_Cycliste_Internationale "Union Cycliste Internationale") team code:* **IGD**[[3]](#cite_note-3)) (stylised as **INEOS Grenadiers**) (formerly **Team Sky** from 2010 to 2019, and **Team Ineos** from 2019 to 2020) is a British professional [cycling team](/wiki/Cycling_team "Cycling team") that competes at the [UCI WorldTeam](/wiki/UCI_WorldTeam "UCI WorldTeam") level. The team is based at the [National Cycling Centre](/wiki/Manchester_Velodrome "Manchester Velodrome") in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), England, with a logistics base in [Deinze](/wiki/Deinze "Deinze"), Belgium.[[4]](#cite_note-guardian.co.uk-4) The team is managed by [British Cycling](/wiki/British_Cycling "British Cycling")'s former performance director, [Sir Dave Brailsford](/wiki/Dave_Brailsford "Dave Brailsford"). The company **Tour Racing Ltd.**[[5]](#cite_note-5) is the corporate entity behind the team in all its iterations, which in line with cycling practice adopts the name of their current primary sponsor.

The team launched in 2010 with the ambition of winning the [Tour de France](/wiki/Tour_de_France "Tour de France") with a British rider within five years,[[6]](#cite_note-TeamSky-timeline-6) a goal achieved in two years when [Bradley Wiggins](/wiki/Bradley_Wiggins "Bradley Wiggins") won the [2012 Tour de France](/wiki/2012_Tour_de_France "2012 Tour de France"), becoming the first British winner in its history, while teammate and fellow Briton [Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome") finished as the runner up and then went on to win the [2013 Tour de France](/wiki/2013_Tour_de_France "2013 Tour de France"). Froome won Sky's third Tour de France title in [2015](/wiki/2015_Tour_de_France "2015 Tour de France"), fourth in [2016](/wiki/2016_Tour_de_France "2016 Tour de France") and fifth in [2017](/wiki/2017_Tour_de_France "2017 Tour de France"). Froome went on to win the [2017 Vuelta a España](/wiki/2017_Vuelta_a_Espa%C3%B1a "2017 Vuelta a España") and the [2018 Giro d'Italia](/wiki/2018_Giro_d%27Italia "2018 Giro d'Italia"), making him the champion of all three Grand Tours at once.

Froome was retrospectively awarded victory in the [2011 Vuelta a España](/wiki/2011_Vuelta_a_Espa%C3%B1a "2011 Vuelta a España"), after the original victor [Juan José Cobo](/wiki/Juan_Jos%C3%A9_Cobo "Juan José Cobo") was stripped of his title due to doping. The team won the [2018 Tour de France](/wiki/2018_Tour_de_France "2018 Tour de France") with [Geraint Thomas](/wiki/Geraint_Thomas "Geraint Thomas"), the [2019 Tour de France](/wiki/2019_Tour_de_France "2019 Tour de France") and [2021 Giro d'Italia](/wiki/2021_Giro_d%27Italia "2021 Giro d'Italia") with [Egan Bernal](/wiki/Egan_Bernal "Egan Bernal"), and the [2020 Giro d'Italia](/wiki/2020_Giro_d%27Italia "2020 Giro d'Italia") with [Tao Geoghegan Hart](/wiki/Tao_Geoghegan_Hart "Tao Geoghegan Hart"), meaning that the team won seven of the eight editions of the Tour de France between 2012 and 2019, with four different riders, and five further grand tours between 2011 and 2021.

Following the decision by British media company [Sky UK](/wiki/Sky_UK "Sky UK") not to renew sponsorship, the team secured financial support from the British chemicals group [Ineos](/wiki/Ineos "Ineos"), with the team thereby renamed as Team Ineos from April 2019.[[7]](#cite_note-change-7) Effective from August 2020 the team was rebranded as Ineos Grenadiers to reflect Ineos Automotive's proposed new off-road vehicle, the [Ineos Grenadier](/wiki/Ineos_Grenadier "Ineos Grenadier").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation")]

The creation of the team was announced on 26 February 2009, with the major sponsorship provided by [BSkyB](/wiki/BSkyB "BSkyB"). The company was searching for a sport in which they could have a positive and wide-ranging impact through their sponsorship. British Cycling first began their relationship with BSkyB in 2008 with a £1 million a year sponsorship for their track cycling team. After a trip to the [Manchester Velodrome](/wiki/Manchester_Velodrome "Manchester Velodrome"), British Cycling's National Cycling Centre, in 2008, then BSkyB chairman [James Murdoch](/wiki/James_Murdoch "James Murdoch") quickly became keen on the sport.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Over the summer of 2008 BSkyB were lobbied by British Cycling and key figures such as [David Brailsford](/wiki/David_Brailsford "David Brailsford") and [Chris Boardman](/wiki/Chris_Boardman "Chris Boardman") to launch a British road cycling team which would compete in road cycling's major events.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) BSkyB agreed to finance the team with €15 million a year, aiming for a British rider to win the Tour de France within five years.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Initially a supermarket was in talks to become a co-sponsor.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

Team Sky's original intention was to build a 25-man squad with a core of British riders[[12]](#cite_note-12) and to nurture the young talent.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The first six riders confirmed were [Geraint Thomas](/wiki/Geraint_Thomas "Geraint Thomas"), [Steve Cummings](/wiki/Steve_Cummings "Steve Cummings"), [Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome"), [Russell Downing](/wiki/Russell_Downing "Russell Downing"), [Ian Stannard](/wiki/Ian_Stannard "Ian Stannard") and [Peter Kennaugh](/wiki/Peter_Kennaugh "Peter Kennaugh"), all British riders.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The ambition to "ensure competitiveness" through other signings, including a number of foreign riders, was expressed.[[4]](#cite_note-guardian.co.uk-4) On 10 September 2009, a further ten riders were confirmed as set to ride for the team. These were [Edvald Boasson Hagen](/wiki/Edvald_Boasson_Hagen "Edvald Boasson Hagen"), [Thomas Löfkvist](/wiki/Thomas_L%C3%B6fkvist "Thomas Löfkvist"), [Kurt Asle Arvesen](/wiki/Kurt_Asle_Arvesen "Kurt Asle Arvesen"), [Simon Gerrans](/wiki/Simon_Gerrans "Simon Gerrans"), [Juan Antonio Flecha](/wiki/Juan_Antonio_Flecha "Juan Antonio Flecha"), [Kjell Carlström](/wiki/Kjell_Carlstr%C3%B6m "Kjell Carlström"), [John-Lee Augustyn](/wiki/John-Lee_Augustyn "John-Lee Augustyn"), [Greg Henderson](/wiki/Greg_Henderson "Greg Henderson"), [Lars Petter Nordhaug](/wiki/Lars_Petter_Nordhaug "Lars Petter Nordhaug"), and [Morris Possoni](/wiki/Morris_Possoni "Morris Possoni").[[15]](#cite_note-15) Further additions to the squad, including [Chris Sutton](/wiki/Christopher_Sutton_(cyclist) "Christopher Sutton (cyclist)") and [Bradley Wiggins](/wiki/Bradley_Wiggins "Bradley Wiggins") from [Garmin–Slipstream](/wiki/Garmin%E2%80%93Slipstream "Garmin–Slipstream"), [Michael Barry](/wiki/Michael_Barry_(cyclist) "Michael Barry (cyclist)"), and [Ben Swift](/wiki/Ben_Swift "Ben Swift") from [Team Katusha](/wiki/Katusha%E2%80%93Alpecin "Katusha–Alpecin") were made before the beginning of the 2010 season.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) Prior to their first season six jerseys were screen tested to ensure visibility whilst filmed in race conditions.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

### 2010: The beginning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2010: The beginning")]

Main article: [2010 Team Sky season](/wiki/2010_Team_Sky_season "2010 Team Sky season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Team_Sky_train_Cancer_Council_Helpline_Classic_2010.jpg/250px-Team_Sky_train_Cancer_Council_Helpline_Classic_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Team_Sky_train_Cancer_Council_Helpline_Classic_2010.jpg)

Sky at their first race, the 2010 [Cancer Council Helpline Classic](/wiki/Cancer_Council_Helpline_Classic "Cancer Council Helpline Classic") in [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide"), Australia.

The team gained a victory in its first race in January 2010, the [Cancer Council Helpline Classic](/wiki/Cancer_Council_Helpline_Classic "Cancer Council Helpline Classic") in [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide"), Australia, a one-day race prior to the Tour Down Under, with Greg Henderson and Chris Sutton taking first and second respectively.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Team Sky's first [ProTour](/wiki/UCI_ProTour "UCI ProTour") event was the [Tour Down Under](/wiki/2010_Tour_Down_Under "2010 Tour Down Under") in January.[[20]](#cite_note-corporate.sky.com-20) The team was awarded a wild-card entry for the [Tour de France](/wiki/2010_Tour_de_France "2010 Tour de France").[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) Team Sky was also invited to compete in the other two of the year's [Grand Tours](/wiki/Grand_Tour_(cycling) "Grand Tour (cycling)"). In February 2010 the team got its first one-day victory when Juan Antonio Flecha won the Belgian semi-classic [Omloop Het Nieuwsblad](/wiki/2010_Omloop_Het_Nieuwsblad "2010 Omloop Het Nieuwsblad") with a solo break.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

On 9 May Wiggins became the first Sky rider to wear the leader's jersey of a Grand Tour when he won the opening prologue of the [Giro d'Italia](/wiki/2010_Giro_d%27Italia "2010 Giro d'Italia"). That same month Ben Swift became the first rider to win an overall classification winning the [Tour de Picardie](/wiki/Tour_de_Picardie "Tour de Picardie"). In the Team's first Tour de France, Geraint Thomas finished second on the cobblestones of stage three, and wore the white jersey as leader of the [young rider classification](/wiki/Young_rider_classification_in_the_Tour_de_France "Young rider classification in the Tour de France").[[26]](#cite_note-26) The Tour was a disappointment for Sky though, with Thomas Löfkvist in 17th overall being their highest placed rider (Wiggins finished in 24th place). Löfkvist led Team Sky at the [Vuelta a España](/wiki/2010_Vuelta_a_Espa%C3%B1a "2010 Vuelta a España"), but the team withdrew after stage seven following the death of soigneur Txema González.[[27]](#cite_note-27) In total Team Sky recorded 22 wins in their debut season, with a further 50 podiums.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### 2011: Grand Tour breakthrough

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2011: Grand Tour breakthrough")]

Main article: [2011 Team Sky season](/wiki/2011_Team_Sky_season "2011 Team Sky season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/2011_Vuelta_a_Espana_-_Stage_19_-_006_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-2011_Vuelta_a_Espana_-_Stage_19_-_006_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2011_Vuelta_a_Espana_-_Stage_19_-_006_(cropped).jpg)

Team Sky's [Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome") (left) at the [2011 Vuelta a España](/wiki/2011_Vuelta_a_Espa%C3%B1a "2011 Vuelta a España"), where he finished second overall. At the time this was Sky's highest in a [Grand Tour](/wiki/Grand_Tour_(cycling) "Grand Tour (cycling)").

Team Sky again began the season in Australia, with Ben Swift winning two stages of the Tour Down Under, and finishing third overall.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Juan Antonio Flecha and Jeremy Hunt finished fourth and sixth respectively in the [Tour of Qatar](/wiki/2011_Tour_of_Qatar "2011 Tour of Qatar") in February, while Boasson Hagen finished first in the points classification and second overall in the [Tour of Oman](/wiki/2011_Tour_of_Oman "2011 Tour of Oman") later that month.[[30]](#cite_note-30) In the Classics season, Wiggins finished third overall in [Paris–Nice](/wiki/2011_Paris%E2%80%93Nice "2011 Paris–Nice")[[31]](#cite_note-31) and Geraint Thomas finished second overall at the [Dwars door Vlaanderen](/wiki/Dwars_door_Vlaanderen "Dwars door Vlaanderen").[[32]](#cite_note-32) The team enjoyed a successful [Tour of California](/wiki/2011_Tour_of_California "2011 Tour of California"), with Ben Swift winning stage two[[33]](#cite_note-33) and Greg Henderson taking victory in stage three.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

At the [Giro d'Italia](/wiki/2011_Giro_d%27Italia "2011 Giro d'Italia"), Thomas Lofkvist was the highest placed Sky rider, finishing 21st overall. The closest the team came to a stage victory was Davide Appollonio's second place on stage 12.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Geraint Thomas secured Sky's first overall victory of the season, by winning the five-day [Bayern-Rundfahrt](/wiki/2011_Bayern_Rundfahrt "2011 Bayern Rundfahrt") race at the end of May.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Boasson Hagen and Wiggins also won stages in the event, with Boasson Hagen claiming the points jersey. In June, Wiggins won the [Critérium du Dauphiné](/wiki/2011_Crit%C3%A9rium_du_Dauphin%C3%A9 "2011 Critérium du Dauphiné"), an important victory for Sky at the time.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

At the [Tour de France](/wiki/2011_Tour_de_France "2011 Tour de France") Sky finished third on stage two, the team time trial. Boasson Hagen secured the team's first ever Tour stage win on stage six. On stage seven, just over 40 km (24.9 mi) from the finish, a crash brought down team leader Wiggins breaking his collarbone and ending his tour.[[38]](#cite_note-38) This prompted a change of approach from Sky, with their riders targeting stage wins. On stage nine, Juan Antonio Flecha was hit by a French media car, which resulted in Flecha colliding with [Vacansoleil–DCM](/wiki/Vacansoleil%E2%80%93DCM "Vacansoleil–DCM") rider [Johnny Hoogerland](/wiki/Johnny_Hoogerland "Johnny Hoogerland"), who crashed into a barbed-wire fence.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Both riders were able to continue despite sustaining injuries in the incident. Geraint Thomas won the combativity award on stage 12.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Boasson Hagen came second to compatriot [Thor Hushovd](/wiki/Thor_Hushovd "Thor Hushovd") ([Garmin–Cervélo](/wiki/EF_Education%E2%80%93EasyPost "EF Education–EasyPost")) on stage 16, before winning the next stage with a solo breakaway. He also finished second on the stage 21 on the [Champs-Élysées](/wiki/Champs-%C3%89lys%C3%A9es_stage_in_the_Tour_de_France "Champs-Élysées stage in the Tour de France"). [Rigoberto Urán](/wiki/Rigoberto_Ur%C3%A1n "Rigoberto Urán") was the highest placed Sky rider with 24th overall, whilst Boasson Hagen's efforts gave the team two stage wins in an eventful Tour.

After the Tour de France, Boasson Hagen's good form continued, as he won the [Vattenfall Cyclassics](/wiki/Vattenfall_Cyclassics "Vattenfall Cyclassics") and took a clean sweep of jerseys at the [Eneco Tour](/wiki/2011_Eneco_Tour "2011 Eneco Tour"). In the third and final [Grand Tour](/wiki/Grand_Tour_(cycling) "Grand Tour (cycling)") of the 2011 season, the [Vuelta a España](/wiki/2011_Vuelta_a_Espa%C3%B1a "2011 Vuelta a España"), Sky riders Froome and Wiggins finished second and third respectively in the general classification.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Chris Sutton won stage two, while Froome won stage 17 of the event.[[43]](#cite_note-43) On 11 October, it was announced that world champion [Mark Cavendish](/wiki/Mark_Cavendish "Mark Cavendish") would be joining the team for the 2012 season, bringing an end to months of speculation.[[44]](#cite_note-44) He was joined by his [HTC–Highroad](/wiki/HTC%E2%80%93Highroad "HTC–Highroad") teammate [Bernhard Eisel](/wiki/Bernhard_Eisel "Bernhard Eisel").[[45]](#cite_note-45)

On 17 July 2019, the UCI awarded Froome the Vuelta title after then winner [Juan José Cobo](/wiki/Juan_Jos%C3%A9_Cobo "Juan José Cobo") was disqualified for abnormalities related to performance-enhancing drugs on his biological passport. This now recognizes Froome as the first British cyclist to win any of the Grand Tours, and the result also elevated Wiggins to second place.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### 2012: Tour de France victory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2012: Tour de France victory")]

Main article: [2012 Team Sky season](/wiki/2012_Team_Sky_season "2012 Team Sky season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Bradley_Wiggins_and_Michael_Rogers%2C_2012_Tour_de_France_finish.jpg/250px-Bradley_Wiggins_and_Michael_Rogers%2C_2012_Tour_de_France_finish.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bradley_Wiggins_and_Michael_Rogers,_2012_Tour_de_France_finish.jpg)

[Bradley Wiggins](/wiki/Bradley_Wiggins "Bradley Wiggins") crosses the finish line on the [Champs-Élysées](/wiki/Champs-%C3%89lys%C3%A9es_stage_in_the_Tour_de_France "Champs-Élysées stage in the Tour de France") with [Michael Rogers](/wiki/Michael_Rogers_(cyclist) "Michael Rogers (cyclist)") to win the [2012 Tour de France](/wiki/2012_Tour_de_France "2012 Tour de France")

In January, Team Sky confirmed their squad for the 2012 season which included eight new signings, Cavendish, Eisel, [Sergio Henao](/wiki/Sergio_Henao "Sergio Henao"), [Danny Pate](/wiki/Danny_Pate "Danny Pate"), [Richie Porte](/wiki/Richie_Porte "Richie Porte"), [Salvatore Puccio](/wiki/Salvatore_Puccio "Salvatore Puccio"), [Luke Rowe](/wiki/Luke_Rowe "Luke Rowe") and [Kanstantsin Sivtsov](/wiki/Kanstantsin_Sivtsov "Kanstantsin Sivtsov").[[47]](#cite_note-47)

At the [Tour Down Under](/wiki/2012_Tour_Down_Under "2012 Tour Down Under") in January, Boasson Hagen won the sprint classification.[[48]](#cite_note-48) In February Sky claimed the team classification at the [Volta ao Algarve](/wiki/2012_Volta_ao_Algarve "2012 Volta ao Algarve"), with Porte winning the overall and Boasson Hagen the points classification.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Wiggins won the overall classifications in the [Paris–Nice](/wiki/2012_Paris%E2%80%93Nice "2012 Paris–Nice") in March[[50]](#cite_note-50) and the [Tour de Romandie](/wiki/2012_Tour_de_Romandie "2012 Tour de Romandie") in April.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

Sky dominated the [Tour de France](/wiki/2012_Tour_de_France "2012 Tour de France") [general classification](/wiki/General_classification_in_the_Tour_de_France "General classification in the Tour de France") with Wiggins first and Froome second overall,[[52]](#cite_note-52) and Cavendish winning three stages including the sprint on the final stage on the [Champs-Élysées](/wiki/Champs-%C3%89lys%C3%A9es_stage_in_the_Tour_de_France "Champs-Élysées stage in the Tour de France") in Paris.[[53]](#cite_note-53) On 9 September, the team achieved their 100th victory with [Lars Petter Nordhaug](/wiki/Lars_Petter_Nordhaug "Lars Petter Nordhaug")'s win in the [Grand Prix Cycliste de Montréal](/wiki/Grand_Prix_Cycliste_de_Montr%C3%A9al "Grand Prix Cycliste de Montréal"). The team also topped the [UCI World Tour](/wiki/UCI_World_Tour "UCI World Tour") teams classification, with a total score of 1767 points.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

In preparation for the 2013 season, the signings of [Vasil Kiryienka](/wiki/Vasil_Kiryienka "Vasil Kiryienka") and [David López García](/wiki/David_L%C3%B3pez_Garc%C3%ADa "David López García") from [Movistar Team](/wiki/Movistar_Team_(men%27s_team) "Movistar Team (men's team)") and 2012 Italian national time trial champion, [Dario Cataldo](/wiki/Dario_Cataldo "Dario Cataldo") from [Omega Pharma–Quick-Step](/wiki/Soudal_Quick-Step "Soudal Quick-Step") were secured. The team also signed [Gabriel Rasch](/wiki/Gabriel_Rasch "Gabriel Rasch"), and on 1 October it was revealed that the team had also signed [Joe Dombrowski](/wiki/Joe_Dombrowski "Joe Dombrowski") and [Ian Boswell](/wiki/Ian_Boswell "Ian Boswell") from the Bontrager-Livestrong team as [neo-pros](/wiki/Glossary_of_cycling#neo-pro "Glossary of cycling").[[55]](#cite_note-55) The year's [Tour of Britain](/wiki/2012_Tour_of_Britain "2012 Tour of Britain") winner, [Jonathan Tiernan-Locke](/wiki/Jonathan_Tiernan-Locke "Jonathan Tiernan-Locke") had signed a two-year deal with the team.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

At the end of the 2012 season, Cavendish moved to Omega Pharma–Quick-Step,[[57]](#cite_note-57) Lars Petter Nordhaug moved to [Blanco Pro Cycling](/wiki/Visma%E2%80%93Lease_a_Bike_(men%27s_team) "Visma–Lease a Bike (men's team)"),[[58]](#cite_note-58) [Davide Appollonio](/wiki/Davide_Appollonio "Davide Appollonio") moved to [Ag2r–La Mondiale](/wiki/Decathlon_CMA_CGM_Team "Decathlon CMA CGM Team"),[[59]](#cite_note-59) Juan Antonio Flecha moved to Vacansoleil–DCM,[[60]](#cite_note-60) [Alex Dowsett](/wiki/Alex_Dowsett "Alex Dowsett") moved to Movistar Team,[[61]](#cite_note-61) and Michael Rogers moved to [Saxo Bank–Tinkoff Bank](/wiki/Tinkoff_(cycling_team) "Tinkoff (cycling team)").[[62]](#cite_note-62) Michael Barry[[63]](#cite_note-63) and [Jeremy Hunt](/wiki/Jeremy_Hunt_(cyclist) "Jeremy Hunt (cyclist)")[[64]](#cite_note-64) both retired.

Doctor Geert Leinders, who had been employed by the Team since 2011, was subject of an internal investigation after allegations of involvement in doping at Blanco Pro Cycling earlier in his career, and on 9 October it was announced he would no longer work for the team.[[65]](#cite_note-65) The impact of the [USADA](/wiki/USADA "USADA") reasoned decision on doping by [Lance Armstrong](/wiki/Lance_Armstrong "Lance Armstrong") and teammates at the [US Postal](/wiki/US_Postal_Service_cycling_team "US Postal Service cycling team") team led Sky to re-inforce its zero tolerance anti-doping policy, with all riders and staff being subjected to internal interviews. Two members of the coaching staff, [Bobby Julich](/wiki/Bobby_Julich "Bobby Julich") and [Steven de Jongh](/wiki/Steven_de_Jongh "Steven de Jongh") were released from their contracts under the policy.[[66]](#cite_note-66) Head Director Sportif [Sean Yates](/wiki/Sean_Yates "Sean Yates") also left the squad in October citing personal reasons,[[67]](#cite_note-67) although the [Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph") linked his departure to past involvement in doping.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

### 2013: The second Tour de France victory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2013: The second Tour de France victory")]

Main article: [2013 Team Sky season](/wiki/2013_Team_Sky_season "2013 Team Sky season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Chris_Froome_TDF2013.jpg/250px-Chris_Froome_TDF2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chris_Froome_TDF2013.jpg)

[Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome") on his way to winning the [2013 Tour de France](/wiki/2013_Tour_de_France "2013 Tour de France")

The 2013 season began with the [Tour Down Under](/wiki/2013_Tour_Down_Under "2013 Tour Down Under"), where Geraint Thomas won stage two and claimed the points classification.[[69]](#cite_note-69) In February Froome won the overall classification, points classification and stage 5 [Tour of Oman](/wiki/2013_Tour_of_Oman "2013 Tour of Oman").[[70]](#cite_note-70) In March Richie Porte won the [Paris–Nice](/wiki/2013_Paris%E2%80%93Nice "2013 Paris–Nice"), including two of the last three stages in the race, the [queen stage](/wiki/Queen_stage "Queen stage") and the concluding time trial.[[71]](#cite_note-71) [Sergio Henao](/wiki/Sergio_Henao "Sergio Henao") claimed his first victory for the team at the [Volta ao Algarve](/wiki/Volta_ao_Algarve "Volta ao Algarve"), whilst Froome took a stage win at [Tirreno–Adriatico](/wiki/Tirreno%E2%80%93Adriatico "Tirreno–Adriatico"). The team then picked up a one–two at the [Critérium International](/wiki/2013_Crit%C3%A9rium_International "2013 Critérium International") with Froome securing victory with a win on the final stage and Porte finishing runner up with a victory in the stage two time trial, also securing the points competition.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

After his victory in the [2012 Tour de France](/wiki/2012_Tour_de_France "2012 Tour de France") [Bradley Wiggins](/wiki/Bradley_Wiggins "Bradley Wiggins") built his early season around targeting the [2013 Giro d'Italia](/wiki/2013_Giro_d%27Italia "2013 Giro d'Italia") and supporting Froome in the Tour de France.[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74) The team took victory in the stage 2 team time trial, culminating in [Salvatore Puccio](/wiki/Salvatore_Puccio "Salvatore Puccio") taking over the pink jersey as leader of the [general classification](/wiki/General_classification_in_the_Giro_d%27Italia "General classification in the Giro d'Italia").[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76) Wiggins was hampered behind a crash on stage 7[[77]](#cite_note-77) and then himself crashed on stage 8.[[78]](#cite_note-78) Wiggins abandoned the Giro due to a chest infection on stage 13.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

Froome followed up with overall wins at the [Tour de Romandie](/wiki/2013_Tour_de_Romandie "2013 Tour de Romandie") in April[[80]](#cite_note-80) and [Critérium du Dauphiné](/wiki/2013_Crit%C3%A9rium_du_Dauphin%C3%A9 "2013 Critérium du Dauphiné") in June.[[81]](#cite_note-81) Boasson Hagen retained his [Tour of Norway](/wiki/2013_Tour_of_Norway "2013 Tour of Norway") title, winning the points classification and stage four of the race in the process.[[82]](#cite_note-82) In July, Froome went on to win the 100th and 2013 edition of the [Tour de France](/wiki/2013_Tour_de_France "2013 Tour de France"); claiming dominant stage victories on the stage eight final climb of [Ax 3 Domaines](/wiki/Ax_3_Domaines "Ax 3 Domaines"), stage 15 to the summit of [Mont Ventoux](/wiki/Mont_Ventoux "Mont Ventoux") and the stage 17 individual time trial. Froome was narrowly beaten to the King of the Mountains prize by Movistar Teams Colombian climber and runner up, [Nairo Quintana](/wiki/Nairo_Quintana "Nairo Quintana").

After the Tour de France, some of the team's key [domestiques](/wiki/Domestiques "Domestiques") secured stage victories at the [Eneco Tour](/wiki/Eneco_Tour "Eneco Tour") ([David Lopez](/wiki/David_L%C3%B3pez_Garc%C3%ADa "David López García")),[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84) and [Vuelta a España](/wiki/2013_Vuelta_a_Espa%C3%B1a "2013 Vuelta a España") ([Vasil Kiryienka](/wiki/Vasil_Kiryienka "Vasil Kiryienka")).[[85]](#cite_note-85) After the disappointment of the Giro, Wiggins returned with a renewed focus on the [2013 UCI Road World Championships](/wiki/2013_UCI_Road_World_Championships "2013 UCI Road World Championships") Individual time trial event.[[86]](#cite_note-86) As part of his build up he won the seventh stage time trial at the [2013 Tour de Pologne](/wiki/2013_Tour_de_Pologne "2013 Tour de Pologne") from [Fabian Cancellara](/wiki/Fabian_Cancellara "Fabian Cancellara") by a winning margin of 56 seconds. The team then recorded their first ever victory in their home stage race, with Wiggins claiming the overall title in the [Tour of Britain](/wiki/2013_Tour_of_Britain "2013 Tour of Britain") winning the stage three time trial in [Knowsley](/wiki/Knowsley_Safari_Park "Knowsley Safari Park") Safari park. Wiggins finished his season with second in the World time trial championships, finishing 46 seconds behind triple world champion, [Tony Martin](/wiki/Tony_Martin_(cyclist) "Tony Martin (cyclist)"), with the team taking the bronze in the team time trial.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

### 2014: Tour failure and the rainbow jersey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2014: Tour failure and the rainbow jersey")]

Main article: [2014 Team Sky season](/wiki/2014_Team_Sky_season "2014 Team Sky season")

On 4 June 2013 it was announced that Australian [Nathan Earle](/wiki/Nathan_Earle_(cyclist) "Nathan Earle (cyclist)") of the Continental team, [Huon Salmon–Genesys Wealth Advisers](/wiki/Bennelong_SwissWellness_Cycling_Team_p/b_Cervelo "Bennelong SwissWellness Cycling Team p/b Cervelo"), had been signed by the team for the 2014 season.[[88]](#cite_note-88) On 1 August 2013, the first day of the cycling transfer window, it was confirmed that [Rigoberto Urán](/wiki/Rigoberto_Ur%C3%A1n "Rigoberto Urán") would move to Omega Pharma–Quick-Step for the 2014 season.[[89]](#cite_note-89) On 22 August it was announced that [Mathew Hayman](/wiki/Mathew_Hayman "Mathew Hayman") would leave the team at the completion of the season and join [Orica–GreenEDGE](/wiki/Team_Jayco%E2%80%93AlUla_(men%27s_team) "Team Jayco–AlUla (men's team)") on a 2-year deal.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

On 6 September it was announced that the Irishman [Philip Deignan](/wiki/Philip_Deignan "Philip Deignan") of [UnitedHealthcare](/wiki/UnitedHealthcare_Pro_Cycling_(men%27s_team) "UnitedHealthcare Pro Cycling (men's team)") would be joining the team after a strong 2013 season.[[91]](#cite_note-91) After heavy speculation at the [road world championships](/wiki/2013_UCI_Road_World_Championships "2013 UCI Road World Championships") it was announced on 1 October that Spanish climber, [Mikel Nieve](/wiki/Mikel_Nieve "Mikel Nieve"), would join the team on a two-year contract, following the closure of his current team ([Euskaltel–Euskadi](/wiki/Euskaltel%E2%80%93Euskadi_(1994%E2%80%932013) "Euskaltel–Euskadi (1994–2013)")) at the end of the season.[[92]](#cite_note-92) On 23 December [Sebastián Henao](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A1n_Henao "Sebastián Henao") (cousin of Team Sky rider [Sergio Henao](/wiki/Sergio_Henao "Sergio Henao")) was announced as the team's final signing for the 2014 season.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

The [2014 season](/wiki/2014_in_men%27s_road_cycling "2014 in men's road cycling") started off well. Froome defended and retained his [Tour of Oman](/wiki/2014_Tour_of_Oman "2014 Tour of Oman") crown and Kennaugh won his first stage race, the [Settimana Internazionale di Coppi e Bartali](/wiki/2014_Settimana_Internazionale_di_Coppi_e_Bartali "2014 Settimana Internazionale di Coppi e Bartali"). Throughout the season, the team endured repeated illnesses and injuries. Geraint Thomas pulled out of the [Paris–Nice](/wiki/2014_Paris%E2%80%93Nice "2014 Paris–Nice") after crashing out on stage seven whilst leading the general classification.[[94]](#cite_note-94) Richie Porte abandoned the [Tirreno–Adriatico](/wiki/2014_Tirreno%E2%80%93Adriatico "2014 Tirreno–Adriatico")[[95]](#cite_note-95) and the [Volta Ciclista a Catalunya](/wiki/2014_Volta_a_Catalunya "2014 Volta a Catalunya"),[[96]](#cite_note-96) resulting in Porte not competing in the [Giro d'Italia](/wiki/2014_Giro_d%27Italia "2014 Giro d'Italia").[[97]](#cite_note-97) A further blow came when Kennaugh pulled out of the Giro, with the team citing illness.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/D%C3%A9part_2e_%C3%A9tape_2014_-_Tarare_%2871%29.JPG/250px-D%C3%A9part_2e_%C3%A9tape_2014_-_Tarare_%2871%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:D%C3%A9part_2e_%C3%A9tape_2014_-_Tarare_(71).JPG)

[Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome") in the leaders jersey at the [2014 Critérium du Dauphiné](/wiki/2014_Crit%C3%A9rium_du_Dauphin%C3%A9 "2014 Critérium du Dauphiné")

In April, the team's fortune began to turn; Froome defended and won the [Tour de Romandie](/wiki/2014_Tour_de_Romandie "2014 Tour de Romandie"),[[99]](#cite_note-99) Wiggins won the overall classification of the [Tour of California](/wiki/2014_Tour_of_California "2014 Tour of California")[[100]](#cite_note-100) and Geraint Thomas won overall classification of the [Bayern-Rundfahrt](/wiki/2014_Bayern_Rundfahrt "2014 Bayern Rundfahrt")[[101]](#cite_note-101) – each taking control of the race by winning the individual time trial stage respectively. Poor luck returned at the [Critérium du Dauphiné](/wiki/2014_Crit%C3%A9rium_du_Dauphin%C3%A9 "2014 Critérium du Dauphiné"), where Froome crashed whilst wearing the leaders jersey,[[102]](#cite_note-102) despite taking three stage wins (two stages for Froome, one for Nieve) Froome finished outside of the top 10, 4' 25" down on race winner [Andrew Talansky](/wiki/Andrew_Talansky "Andrew Talansky").[[103]](#cite_note-103)

In July, Froome returned to racing to defend his [Tour de France](/wiki/2013_Tour_de_France "2013 Tour de France") victory, hopeful of overall victory. Froome crashed twice on stage four and abandoned the race, having also crashed the day before, with [Xabier Zandio](/wiki/Xabier_Zandio "Xabier Zandio") abandoning on the sixth stage of the race.[[104]](#cite_note-104) As a result, Richie Porte inherited team leadership duties but lost time in both the Alpine and Pyrenean stages. The team's highest rider on general classification was Nieve in 18th position, 46 minutes 31 seconds behind the winner, [Vincenzo Nibali](/wiki/Vincenzo_Nibali "Vincenzo Nibali")[[105]](#cite_note-105) this marked one of the worst performances of the team at the [Tour de France](/wiki/Tour_de_France "Tour de France"). Not selected to ride the Tour, Kennaugh went on to take his second overall race victory at the [Tour of Austria](/wiki/2014_Tour_of_Austria "2014 Tour of Austria"), taking the points classification in the process.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

After abandoning the Tour de France, Froome announced he would ride the [2014 Vuelta a España](/wiki/2014_Vuelta_a_Espa%C3%B1a "2014 Vuelta a España") where he finished in second position, finishing one minute ten seconds, behind the winner [Alberto Contador](/wiki/Alberto_Contador "Alberto Contador"). Froome was awarded the overall combativity award for the entire race.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/B_Wiggins_PN2015.png/250px-B_Wiggins_PN2015.png)](/wiki/File:B_Wiggins_PN2015.png)

[Bradley Wiggins](/wiki/Bradley_Wiggins "Bradley Wiggins") wearing the [rainbow](/wiki/Rainbow_jersey "Rainbow jersey") skinsuit at the [2015 Paris–Nice](/wiki/2015_Paris%E2%80%93Nice "2015 Paris–Nice")

In September, Wiggins returned to the [Tour of Britain](/wiki/2014_Tour_of_Britain "2014 Tour of Britain") with the stated aim of defending his title. He finished third overall, winning the final day time trial by eight seconds from [Sylvain Chavanel](/wiki/Sylvain_Chavanel "Sylvain Chavanel"). Wiggins returned to action later in September at the [road world championships](/wiki/2014_UCI_Road_World_Championships "2014 UCI Road World Championships"), again with the aim of winning the time trial event. Wiggins won the time trial by over 25 seconds from perennial opponent, [Tony Martin](/wiki/Tony_Martin_(cyclist) "Tony Martin (cyclist)").[[107]](#cite_note-107) Wiggins won Team Sky's first ever rainbow jersey.

On 28 July 2014, the team announced that Thomas had signed a two-year contract extension, keeping him at the team until the end of the 2016 season.[[108]](#cite_note-108) In September Swift signed a two-year contract extension.[[109]](#cite_note-109) On 13 August 2014, *Cyclingnews.com* reported that [Edvald Boasson Hagen](/wiki/Edvald_Boasson_Hagen "Edvald Boasson Hagen") would not renew his contract and would leave the team at the end of the season.[[110]](#cite_note-110)
After the cycling World Championships, Sky announced that they had signed [Leopold König](/wiki/Leopold_K%C3%B6nig "Leopold König"), [Nicolas Roche](/wiki/Nicolas_Roche "Nicolas Roche"), [Wout Poels](/wiki/Wout_Poels "Wout Poels"), and [Andrew Fenn](/wiki/Andrew_Fenn "Andrew Fenn"), with [Lars Petter Nordhaug](/wiki/Lars_Petter_Nordhaug "Lars Petter Nordhaug") rejoining the team after two years at Belkin.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

On 1 October 2014, it was announced that [Dario Cataldo](/wiki/Dario_Cataldo "Dario Cataldo") would leave the team at the end of the season to join [Astana](/wiki/XDS_Astana_Team "XDS Astana Team").[[112]](#cite_note-112) On 24 October, the team announced the signing of their sixth rider, [Elia Viviani](/wiki/Elia_Viviani "Elia Viviani").[[113]](#cite_note-113) American climber [Joe Dombrowski](/wiki/Joe_Dombrowski "Joe Dombrowski") also left Team Sky to join [Cannondale–Garmin](/wiki/EF_Education%E2%80%93EasyPost "EF Education–EasyPost").[[114]](#cite_note-114) On 5 January 2015, Wiggins signed a contract extension with the team up until and including the [2015 Paris–Roubaix](/wiki/2015_Paris%E2%80%93Roubaix "2015 Paris–Roubaix").[[115]](#cite_note-115)

### 2015: The Third Tour de France and another World Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2015: The Third Tour de France and another World Championship")]

Main article: [2015 Team Sky season](/wiki/2015_Team_Sky_season "2015 Team Sky season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Chris_Froome%2C_TDF_2015%2C_%C3%A9tape_13%2C_Montgiscard.jpg/250px-Chris_Froome%2C_TDF_2015%2C_%C3%A9tape_13%2C_Montgiscard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chris_Froome,_TDF_2015,_%C3%A9tape_13,_Montgiscard.jpg)

Froome in the leaders jersey on stage thirteen of the [2015 Tour de France](/wiki/2015_Tour_de_France "2015 Tour de France")

On 8 January, [Richie Porte](/wiki/Richie_Porte "Richie Porte") scored the team's first victory of the season by winning the Australian National Time Trial championships[[116]](#cite_note-116) and went on to record the team's first stage win at the [Tour Down Under](/wiki/2015_Tour_Down_Under "2015 Tour Down Under"). [Elia Viviani](/wiki/Elia_Viviani "Elia Viviani") scored his first win for the team, taking sprint victory on stage two of the [Dubai Tour](/wiki/2015_Dubai_Tour "2015 Dubai Tour").

In February the team dominated the [Vuelta a Andalucía](/wiki/2015_Vuelta_a_Andaluc%C3%ADa "2015 Vuelta a Andalucía") and [Volta ao Algarve](/wiki/2015_Volta_ao_Algarve "2015 Volta ao Algarve") with both Froome and Thomas taking both overall wins respectively. At the end of February Stannard scored the team second classic, taking a second successive victory at [Omloop Het Nieuwsblad](/wiki/2015_Omloop_Het_Nieuwsblad "2015 Omloop Het Nieuwsblad"). The victory was made more impressive as Stannard made the four-man selection with three [Etixx–Quick-Step](/wiki/Soudal_Quick-Step "Soudal Quick-Step") riders; Boonen, Terpstra and Vandenbergh.[[117]](#cite_note-117)[[118]](#cite_note-118)

The team's next victory came at [Paris–Nice](/wiki/2015_Paris%E2%80%93Nice "2015 Paris–Nice") where Porte led a team one-two (along with Thomas) at the summit finish of Croix de Chaubouret.[[119]](#cite_note-119)[[120]](#cite_note-120)[[121]](#cite_note-121) Porte won the stage 7 time trial to the summit of [Col d'Èze](/wiki/Col_d%27%C3%88ze "Col d'Èze").[[122]](#cite_note-122)[[123]](#cite_note-123) In the same week, [Wout Poels](/wiki/Wout_Poels "Wout Poels") recorded his first win for the team when he secured victory on the fifth stage of [Tirreno–Adriatico](/wiki/2015_Tirreno%E2%80%93Adriatico "2015 Tirreno–Adriatico") to [Castelraimondo](/wiki/Castelraimondo "Castelraimondo").

In late March, Thomas emerged victorious in [E3 Harelbeke](/wiki/2015_E3_Harelbeke "2015 E3 Harelbeke") after attacking his co-breakaway companions, [Zdeněk Štybar](/wiki/Zden%C4%9Bk_%C5%A0tybar "Zdeněk Štybar") and [Peter Sagan](/wiki/Peter_Sagan "Peter Sagan"), and soloing to victory.[[124]](#cite_note-124)[[125]](#cite_note-125) [Ben Swift](/wiki/Ben_Swift "Ben Swift") then won the second stage of [Settimana Internazionale di Coppi e Bartali](/wiki/Settimana_Internazionale_di_Coppi_e_Bartali "Settimana Internazionale di Coppi e Bartali") the same day[[126]](#cite_note-126)[[127]](#cite_note-127) and Richie Porte moved into the lead and eventually won the [Volta a Catalunya](/wiki/2015_Volta_a_Catalunya "2015 Volta a Catalunya")[[128]](#cite_note-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130) Victory in Catalunya represented Porte's second overall win of the season and the fourth for the team.[[131]](#cite_note-131)[[132]](#cite_note-132)

In April, Bradley Wiggins won his final time trial for the team at the [Three Days of De Panne](/wiki/2015_Three_Days_of_De_Panne "2015 Three Days of De Panne").[[133]](#cite_note-133) Later in the same week, Bradley Wiggins retired from the team and joined his own [WIGGINS](/wiki/Team_Wiggins_Le_Col "Team Wiggins Le Col") team, allowing him to focus on the [2016 Olympic Games](/wiki/2016_Olympic_Games "2016 Olympic Games").[[134]](#cite_note-134) In late April Porte won the [Giro del Trentino](/wiki/2015_Giro_del_Trentino "2015 Giro del Trentino")[[135]](#cite_note-135) taking a decisive stage victory on the queen stage summit finish to [Brentonico](/wiki/Brentonico "Brentonico").[[136]](#cite_note-136) The team then rounded off a successful April by taking victory in the [Team Time Trial](/wiki/Team_Time_Trial "Team Time Trial") at the [Tour de Romandie](/wiki/2015_Tour_de_Romandie "2015 Tour de Romandie"), placing Geraint Thomas in the yellow leaders jersey[[137]](#cite_note-Cycling_News-137) whilst new signing [Wout Poels](/wiki/Wout_Poels "Wout Poels") underwent surgery on a broken shoulder bone courtesy of his crash at [La Flèche Wallonne](/wiki/2015_La_Fl%C3%A8che_Wallonne "2015 La Flèche Wallonne").[[138]](#cite_note-138)

The team began May with success; [Lars Petter Nordhaug](/wiki/Lars_Petter_Nordhaug "Lars Petter Nordhaug") took the opening stage win at the inaugural [Tour de Yorkshire](/wiki/2015_Tour_de_Yorkshire "2015 Tour de Yorkshire"), whilst [Ben Swift](/wiki/Ben_Swift "Ben Swift") crashed out[[139]](#cite_note-139) later requiring surgery.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

The team entered the [2015 Giro d'Italia](/wiki/2015_Giro_d%27Italia "2015 Giro d'Italia") with Porte as team leader with the aim of winning the [general classification](/wiki/General_classification_in_the_Giro_d%27Italia "General classification in the Giro d'Italia"). After limiting the time loss in the stage 1 team time trial [Elia Viviani](/wiki/Elia_Viviani "Elia Viviani") secured the first win for the team in a Grand Tour since the 2013 Vuelta a España on stage 2, also taking over the *Maglia rossa*. On stage 10 Porte lost 47 seconds,[[141]](#cite_note-141) docked two minutes fine for accepting outside intervention,[[142]](#cite_note-barryryan-142) resulting in Porte dropping down to 12th on the general classification.[[143]](#cite_note-143) Porte lost more time on the uphill finish on stage 12,[[144]](#cite_note-144) a further two minutes on stage 13,[[145]](#cite_note-145) and 27 minutes on stage 15. He then abandoned on the second rest day.[[146]](#cite_note-146)

Chris Froome returned to action at the [Critérium du Dauphiné](/wiki/2015_Crit%C3%A9rium_du_Dauphin%C3%A9 "2015 Critérium du Dauphiné"), as part of his build up for the [Tour de France](/wiki/2015_Tour_de_France "2015 Tour de France"), and the team won three stages and took the overall title for the fourth time. [Peter Kennaugh](/wiki/Peter_Kennaugh "Peter Kennaugh") opened the team's account taking the victory on stage one, just in front of the bunch sprint finish.[[147]](#cite_note-147) Froome went on to dominate the final two summit finish stages, taking victory at Montée du Bettex (stage 7)[[148]](#cite_note-148) and Modane Valfréjus (stage 8)[[149]](#cite_note-149) giving him a lead of 10 seconds over [Tejay van Garderen](/wiki/Tejay_van_Garderen "Tejay van Garderen").

The team went into the [2015 Tour de France](/wiki/2015_Tour_de_France "2015 Tour de France") with their "strongest team ever"[[150]](#cite_note-150) After a strong performance on the [Mur de Huy](/wiki/Mur_de_Huy "Mur de Huy") Froome took over the race lead, and general classification by one second over [Tony Martin](/wiki/Tony_Martin_(cyclist) "Tony Martin (cyclist)"). As the Tour entered the second week of racing stage 10 saw the first mountains stage, the summit finish of La Pierre-Saint-Martin, where Froome went on to take the stage win, putting significant time into his general classification rivals.[[151]](#cite_note-151) During the remainder of the race the team faced intense scrutiny regarding their dominant performances; Porte was punched in the ribs by a spectator in the Pyrenees,[[152]](#cite_note-152) and Froome had urine thrown at him by another spectator.[[153]](#cite_note-153)

On the first rest of the Tour de France, Porte confirmed he would leave the team at the end of the season.[[154]](#cite_note-154) This would later, in August, be confirmed to be [CCC Pro Team](/wiki/CCC_Pro_Team "CCC Pro Team").[[155]](#cite_note-155) The team signed [Alex Peters](/wiki/Alex_Peters "Alex Peters") and [Tao Geoghegan Hart](/wiki/Tao_Geoghegan_Hart "Tao Geoghegan Hart") as stagiares for the remainder of the season, with the former also signing for two years.[[156]](#cite_note-156) In September, [Mikel Landa](/wiki/Mikel_Landa "Mikel Landa") confirmed his move to the team for the 2016 season,[[157]](#cite_note-157) with [Mikel Nieve](/wiki/Mikel_Nieve "Mikel Nieve") also signing a two-year extension with the team.[[158]](#cite_note-158) Later, in the same month the team then signed their second neo-pro, [Gianni Moscon](/wiki/Gianni_Moscon "Gianni Moscon"),[[159]](#cite_note-159) [Michał Gołaś](/wiki/Micha%C5%82_Go%C5%82a%C5%9B "Michał Gołaś"),[[160]](#cite_note-160) [Danny van Poppel](/wiki/Danny_van_Poppel "Danny van Poppel"),[[161]](#cite_note-161) [Beñat Intxausti](/wiki/Be%C3%B1at_Intxausti "Beñat Intxausti")[[162]](#cite_note-162) and the 2014 World Road Race champion [Michał Kwiatkowski](/wiki/Micha%C5%82_Kwiatkowski "Michał Kwiatkowski").[[163]](#cite_note-163)

On 28 September the team confirmed that Nathan Earle, Bernhard Eisel, Danny Pate, Richie Porte, Kanstantsin Siutsou and Chris Sutton would be leaving the team. The team also confirmed that Ian Boswell, Philip Deignan, Sebastián Henao, Peter Kennaugh, Vasil Kiryienka, Christian Knees, Mikel Nieve, Luke Rowe, Ian Stannard and Xabier Zandio would be staying after signing new contract extensions.[[164]](#cite_note-164)

Later in September Sky retained the [World Time Trial Championships](/wiki/2015_UCI_Road_World_Championships_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_time_trial "2015 UCI Road World Championships – Men's time trial") in Richmond when [Vasil Kiryienka](/wiki/Vasil_Kiryienka "Vasil Kiryienka") won by nine seconds from [Adriano Malori](/wiki/Adriano_Malori "Adriano Malori").[[165]](#cite_note-165)

### 2016: First Monument win and the fourth Tour victory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2016: First Monument win and the fourth Tour victory")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Geraint_Thomas_jaune_Madone_d%27Utelle_2016.JPG/250px-Geraint_Thomas_jaune_Madone_d%27Utelle_2016.JPG)](/wiki/File:Geraint_Thomas_jaune_Madone_d%27Utelle_2016.JPG)

[Geraint Thomas](/wiki/Geraint_Thomas "Geraint Thomas") at the [2016 Paris–Nice](/wiki/2016_Paris%E2%80%93Nice "2016 Paris–Nice"), where he would claim the team's fourth overall win in the past five seasons.

Main article: [2016 Team Sky season](/wiki/2016_Team_Sky_season "2016 Team Sky season")

With Chris Froome delaying the start of his season a number of key deluxe-domestiques were afforded opportunities to aim for race victories at the start of the season. Peter Kennaugh took the team's first one-day race win at the second [Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race](/wiki/Cadel_Evans_Great_Ocean_Road_Race "Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race"),[[166]](#cite_note-166) whilst Wout Poels claimed his first overall GC victory at the [Volta a la Comunitat Valenciana](/wiki/Volta_a_la_Comunitat_Valenciana "Volta a la Comunitat Valenciana"),[[167]](#cite_note-167)[[168]](#cite_note-168) in the process taking two stages, the mountains and the points classifications. Froome returned towards the end of the Australian cycling season to claim the team's first ever GC win at the [Herald Sun Tour](/wiki/Herald_Sun_Tour "Herald Sun Tour").[[169]](#cite_note-169) Geraint Thomas enjoyed a successful start to his 2016 campaign, notching up overall victories at [Volta ao Algarve](/wiki/Volta_ao_Algarve "Volta ao Algarve")[[170]](#cite_note-170) (for the second successive year) and [Paris–Nice](/wiki/Paris%E2%80%93Nice "Paris–Nice").[[171]](#cite_note-171) Thomas' victory in the French stage race means that Sky have won four of the past five editions.

Sky entered the spring classics campaign with the perennial aim of claiming their first monument race. New recruit, [Michał Kwiatkowski](/wiki/Micha%C5%82_Kwiatkowski "Michał Kwiatkowski"), took his first victory for the team in the [E3 Harelbeke](/wiki/E3_Harelbeke "E3 Harelbeke") semi-classic.[[172]](#cite_note-172) As the classics campaign progressed the team showed great consistency, but fell short of their goal again, taking second in [Milan–San Remo](/wiki/Milan%E2%80%93San_Remo "Milan–San Remo"),[[173]](#cite_note-173) fifth at the [Tour of Flanders](/wiki/Tour_of_Flanders_(men%27s_race) "Tour of Flanders (men's race)")[[174]](#cite_note-174) and third at [Paris–Roubaix](/wiki/Paris%E2%80%93Roubaix "Paris–Roubaix").[[175]](#cite_note-175) Upon entering the Ardennes classics the team pinned their hopes on Kwiatkowski at [La Flèche Wallonne](/wiki/La_Fl%C3%A8che_Wallonne "La Flèche Wallonne"), to no avail.[[176]](#cite_note-176) Entering the final monument of the spring, [Liège–Bastogne–Liège](/wiki/2016_Li%C3%A8ge%E2%80%93Bastogne%E2%80%93Li%C3%A8ge "2016 Liège–Bastogne–Liège"), the team again led with Kwiatkowski. Sky finally achieved their coveted monument win, but it wasn't Kwiatkowski, but rather, domestique [Wout Poels](/wiki/Wout_Poels "Wout Poels") who emerged victorious on a day with poor weather conditions and enduring snow.[[177]](#cite_note-177)

New recruit [Mikel Landa](/wiki/Mikel_Landa "Mikel Landa") claimed his first overall win for team – taking the GC at the [2016 Giro del Trentino](/wiki/2016_Giro_del_Trentino "2016 Giro del Trentino") only a week after claiming his first stage win for the team. Landa's stated aim for the first half of the season is the [Giro d'Italia](/wiki/Giro_d%27Italia "Giro d'Italia").[[178]](#cite_note-178) Froome added to Sky's race win total by successfully defending his [Critérium du Dauphiné](/wiki/2016_Crit%C3%A9rium_du_Dauphin%C3%A9 "2016 Critérium du Dauphiné") title after taking the overall lead with a victory on stage 5 to [Vaujany](/wiki/Vaujany "Vaujany"), becoming the fifth rider to win the overall title three times.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Tour_de_France_2016%2C_froome_%2827979590983%29.jpg/250px-Tour_de_France_2016%2C_froome_%2827979590983%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tour_de_France_2016,_froome_(27979590983).jpg)

[Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome") during a [time trial](/wiki/Time_trial "Time trial") on his way to victory at the [2016 Tour de France](/wiki/2016_Tour_de_France "2016 Tour de France")

The team went into the [2016 Tour de France](/wiki/2016_Tour_de_France "2016 Tour de France") with what was without question an even stronger squad than the previous year. A week into the race on stage 8, Froome took many by surprise by attacking on the descent of the [Col de Peyresourde](/wiki/Col_de_Peyresourde "Col de Peyresourde") which caught his main rivals off guard, resulting in a solo victory into [Bagnères-de-Luchon](/wiki/Bagn%C3%A8res-de-Luchon "Bagnères-de-Luchon") to take the yellow jersey. On stage 11 to [Montpellier](/wiki/Montpellier "Montpellier") in crosswinds, he gained more time as part of a 4-man breakaway in the final 12 kilometres of the stage alongside green jersey wearer [Peter Sagan](/wiki/Peter_Sagan "Peter Sagan"), Sagan's Tinkoff teammate [Maciej Bodnar](/wiki/Maciej_Bodnar "Maciej Bodnar"), and Froome's own teammate Geraint Thomas, placing second to Sagan in a sprint finish.

On stage 12 to [Mont Ventoux](/wiki/Mont_Ventoux "Mont Ventoux"), which was shortened to Chalet Reynard due to high winds, Froome was involved in a crash involving a stopped motorbike that also sent [Richie Porte](/wiki/Richie_Porte "Richie Porte") and [Bauke Mollema](/wiki/Bauke_Mollema "Bauke Mollema") down. With no usable bike, Froome had no choice but to run partway up the mountain until he was able to get a spare bike from his team car. Provisionally he had fallen to sixth place overall which would have given the yellow jersey to [Adam Yates](/wiki/Adam_Yates "Adam Yates"), however race officials decided to give him and Porte the same time as Mollema, ensuring Froome would hold on to his first overall position.

Superb performances in both the individual time trials (second on stage 13 to [La Caverne du Pont-d'Arc](/wiki/Caverne_du_Pont-d%27Arc "Caverne du Pont-d'Arc") and winner of stage 18 to [Megève](/wiki/Meg%C3%A8ve "Megève")) allowed Froome to gain even more time on his rivals, and despite a crash on stage 19 to [Saint-Gervais-les-Bains](/wiki/Saint-Gervais-les-Bains "Saint-Gervais-les-Bains") where he finished the stage on Thomas' bike with support from Wout Poels, it was enough for him to win the race overall 4:05 ahead of second-placed [Romain Bardet](/wiki/Romain_Bardet "Romain Bardet"), becoming the first British rider to win the Tour on three occasions, the fourth rider to become a 3-time winner (joining [Philippe Thys](/wiki/Philippe_Thys_(cyclist) "Philippe Thys (cyclist)"), [Louison Bobet](/wiki/Louison_Bobet "Louison Bobet") and [Greg LeMond](/wiki/Greg_LeMond "Greg LeMond")), and the first since [Miguel Induráin](/wiki/Miguel_Indur%C3%A1in "Miguel Induráin") in 1995 to successfully defend his title. It was also the first time the team finished a Grand Tour with all nine riders intact.

Sky started off their [2016 Vuelta a España](/wiki/2016_Vuelta_a_Espa%C3%B1a "2016 Vuelta a España") campaign in late August by winning the team time trial on stage 1 and holding the red jersey for 2 days before surrendering it on stage 3, where Chris Froome took a 4th-place finish at [Mirador de Ézaro](/wiki/Dumbr%C3%ADa "Dumbría") to elevate himself into third place in the overall standings. He then got the victory on stage 11 at [Peña Cabarga](/wiki/Medio_Cudeyo "Medio Cudeyo"), the very same mountain where he got his first-ever Grand Tour stage win in 2011, and put himself into second overall just under a minute behind race leader [Nairo Quintana](/wiki/Nairo_Quintana "Nairo Quintana").

A series of early attacks by Quntana and [Alberto Contador](/wiki/Alberto_Contador "Alberto Contador") on stage 15 to [Aramon Formigal](/wiki/Aramon_Formigal "Aramon Formigal") blew the race apart, causing Froome to be isolated from his teammates and to lose more than 2+1⁄2 minutes on his rival. He gained most of that time back on the stage 19 time trial to [Calp](/wiki/Calp "Calp") with a dominant performance, but was unable to make up the difference in the penultimate stage, finishing second overall in the end by just 1:23 behind Quintana. It was Froome's second Grand Tour podium following his win at the Tour de France, and just coming off of winning the bronze medal in the time trial at the Olympic Games in Rio.

In August 2016 the news broke that the team had signed Olympic Team Pursuit champion, [Owain Doull](/wiki/Owain_Doull "Owain Doull") from [WIGGINS](/wiki/Team_Wiggins_Le_Col "Team Wiggins Le Col") and [Tao Geoghegan Hart](/wiki/Tao_Geoghegan_Hart "Tao Geoghegan Hart") from [Axeon–Hagens Berman](/wiki/Hagens_Berman_Jayco "Hagens Berman Jayco")[[179]](#cite_note-179)[[180]](#cite_note-cyclingweekly.co.uk-180) Later, in the same month, the team confirmed their third signing of the season – Polish rider [Łukasz Wiśniowski](/wiki/%C5%81ukasz_Wi%C5%9Bniowski "Łukasz Wiśniowski") from [Etixx–Quick-Step](/wiki/Soudal_Quick-Step "Soudal Quick-Step").[[181]](#cite_note-181) For the 2017 season [Nicolas Roche](/wiki/Nicolas_Roche "Nicolas Roche") will leave for [BMC Racing Team](/wiki/CCC_Pro_Team "CCC Pro Team"), as well as [Andy Fenn](/wiki/Andy_Fenn "Andy Fenn"), [Lars Petter Nordhaug](/wiki/Lars_Petter_Nordhaug "Lars Petter Nordhaug") (both to new Irish team [Aqua Blue Sport](/wiki/Aqua_Blue_Sport "Aqua Blue Sport")) and [Leopold König](/wiki/Leopold_K%C3%B6nig "Leopold König") (to [Bora–Hansgrohe](/wiki/Red_Bull%E2%80%93Bora%E2%80%93Hansgrohe "Red Bull–Bora–Hansgrohe")).[[180]](#cite_note-cyclingweekly.co.uk-180) [Luke Rowe](/wiki/Luke_Rowe "Luke Rowe"), [Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome"), [Christian Knees](/wiki/Christian_Knees "Christian Knees"), [Salvatore Puccio](/wiki/Salvatore_Puccio "Salvatore Puccio") and [Geraint Thomas](/wiki/Geraint_Thomas "Geraint Thomas") have renewed with the team.[[180]](#cite_note-cyclingweekly.co.uk-180)

In September the team announced the signing of [Kenny Elissonde](/wiki/Kenny_Elissonde "Kenny Elissonde") from [Groupama–FDJ United](/wiki/Groupama%E2%80%93FDJ_United "Groupama–FDJ United") on a two-year deal.[[182]](#cite_note-182) On 30 September, [Wout Poels](/wiki/Wout_Poels "Wout Poels") signed a three-year contract extension with the team.[[183]](#cite_note-183) [Elia Viviani](/wiki/Elia_Viviani "Elia Viviani") signed a new 2-year deal on 26 October.[[184]](#cite_note-184) The team announced on 27 October that it had signed [Diego Rosa](/wiki/Diego_Rosa_(cyclist) "Diego Rosa (cyclist)") from [XDS Astana Team](/wiki/XDS_Astana_Team "XDS Astana Team") on a 3-year deal,[[185]](#cite_note-185) and on 8 November announced the signing of Doull's WIGGINS teammate [Jonathan Dibben](/wiki/Jonathan_Dibben "Jonathan Dibben").[[186]](#cite_note-186)

The team finished the 2016 season 3rd overall in the UCI World Tour team rankings, with Froome as its highest-ranked rider, finishing 3rd overall in the individual rider ranking.[[187]](#cite_note-187)

### 2017: The Grand Tour double and Milan–San Remo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2017: The Grand Tour double and Milan–San Remo")]

Main article: [2017 Team Sky season](/wiki/2017_Team_Sky_season "2017 Team Sky season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Finishing_line_2017_Milan-Sanremo.jpg/250px-Finishing_line_2017_Milan-Sanremo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Finishing_line_2017_Milan-Sanremo.jpg)

[Michał Kwiatkowski](/wiki/Micha%C5%82_Kwiatkowski "Michał Kwiatkowski") winning the sprint at the [2017 Milan–San Remo](/wiki/2017_Milan%E2%80%93San_Remo "2017 Milan–San Remo")

Sky started the 2017 season with three stage wins at the [Herald Sun Tour](/wiki/2017_Herald_Sun_Tour "2017 Herald Sun Tour") as well as winning the teams classification, with new team member [Kenny Elissonde](/wiki/Kenny_Elissonde "Kenny Elissonde") finishing on the podium in 3rd place and Chris Froome finishing sixth overall. The team's first race win of the season came at [Strade Bianche](/wiki/2017_Strade_Bianche "2017 Strade Bianche") when [Michał Kwiatkowski](/wiki/Micha%C5%82_Kwiatkowski "Michał Kwiatkowski") took a solo victory after attacking 15 kilometres from the finish.[[188]](#cite_note-188) [Geraint Thomas](/wiki/Geraint_Thomas "Geraint Thomas") took a stage win at [Tirreno–Adriatico](/wiki/2017_Tirreno%E2%80%93Adriatico "2017 Tirreno–Adriatico") and held the leader's jersey for one day, also taking the overall victory and one stage win at the [Tour of the Alps](/wiki/2017_Tour_of_the_Alps "2017 Tour of the Alps").[[189]](#cite_note-CN8-189)

Sky won [Paris–Nice](/wiki/2017_Paris%E2%80%93Nice "2017 Paris–Nice") for the fifth time in sixth years courtesy of [Sergio Henao](/wiki/Sergio_Henao "Sergio Henao"), who won the race overall by just two seconds over Alberto Contador.[[189]](#cite_note-CN8-189) Kwiatkowski added to Sky's win total by winning [Milan–San Remo](/wiki/2017_Milan%E2%80%93San_Remo "2017 Milan–San Remo") in a three-up sprint, giving the team its second Monument win.[[190]](#cite_note-190) Neo-pro [Jonathan Dibben](/wiki/Jonathan_Dibben "Jonathan Dibben") won the individual time trial stage at the [Tour of California](/wiki/2017_Tour_of_California "2017 Tour of California"),[[191]](#cite_note-191) the team winning the team classification with [Tao Geoghegan Hart](/wiki/Tao_Geoghegan_Hart "Tao Geoghegan Hart") and [Ian Boswell](/wiki/Ian_Boswell "Ian Boswell") placing in the final top ten overall.[[192]](#cite_note-192)

Thomas and [Mikel Landa](/wiki/Mikel_Landa "Mikel Landa") were appointed the team's joint leaders for the [Giro d'Italia](/wiki/2017_Giro_d%27Italia "2017 Giro d'Italia").[[193]](#cite_note-193) At one point in the race, Thomas was sitting second in the general classification, however on the 9th stage to Blockhaus a fair chunk of the peloton was involved in a crash involving a stopped motorbike, which took down Thomas, Landa and most of the other members of the team.[[194]](#cite_note-194) Thomas attempted a comeback after placing second in the following day's time trial,[[195]](#cite_note-195) but the extent of his injuries were as such that he abandoned the race prior to stage 11.[[196]](#cite_note-196) Landa continued as sole leader, taking several top-3 finishes on several mountain stages before winning stage 19 to [Piancavallo](/wiki/Piancavallo "Piancavallo").[[197]](#cite_note-197) He went on to win the mountains classification and the super-combativity prize,[[198]](#cite_note-198) also winning the [Cima Coppi](/wiki/Cima_Coppi "Cima Coppi") for being the first to cross the summit of the [Stelvio Pass](/wiki/Stelvio_Pass "Stelvio Pass") on stage 16.[[199]](#cite_note-199)

The team then participated in the inaugural [Hammer Series](/wiki/Hammer_Series "Hammer Series"), a three-day event pitting teams against each other in various skills competitions. Impressive performances by Tao Geoghegan Hart in the climbing event and Elia Viviani in the sprint event gave the team enough points to take the overall lead. In the team time trial-style Chase event, they held on by one second over Team Sunweb to win the entire competition.[[200]](#cite_note-200) Froome returned to action at the [Critérium du Dauphiné](/wiki/2017_Crit%C3%A9rium_du_Dauphin%C3%A9 "2017 Critérium du Dauphiné") and finished fourth overall, just one second off the podium.[[201]](#cite_note-201) Teammate [Peter Kennaugh](/wiki/Peter_Kennaugh "Peter Kennaugh") won the penultimate stage at [Alpe d'Huez](/wiki/Alpe_d%27Huez "Alpe d'Huez").[[202]](#cite_note-202)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Tour_de_France_2017%2C_Stage_2_%2835546634611%29.jpg/250px-Tour_de_France_2017%2C_Stage_2_%2835546634611%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tour_de_France_2017,_Stage_2_(35546634611).jpg)

Team Sky prior to stage 2 of the [2017 Tour de France](/wiki/2017_Tour_de_France "2017 Tour de France") as leaders of the team classification

The team then went into the [2017 Tour de France](/wiki/2017_Tour_de_France "2017 Tour de France") with yet another strong lineup and the goal to help Froome achieve his fourth overall victory.[[203]](#cite_note-203) In the opening prologue stage in [Düsseldorf](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorf "Düsseldorf") the team placed four riders within the top eight led by Geraint Thomas, who became the first Welsh rider to wear the yellow jersey. He was followed by [Vasil Kiryienka](/wiki/Vasil_Kiryienka "Vasil Kiryienka") in third, Froome in 6th and Kwiatkowski in eighth – Froome putting between 35 seconds and nearly a minute into most of his general classification rivals. On stage five to [La Planche des Belles Filles](/wiki/La_Planche_des_Belles_Filles "La Planche des Belles Filles"), Froome took third place behind stage winner [Fabio Aru](/wiki/Fabio_Aru "Fabio Aru"), moving him into yellow and Thomas to second place.

The 1–2 placing on GC held until stage nine to [Chambéry](/wiki/Chamb%C3%A9ry "Chambéry"), when Thomas was involved in a crash on the descent of the Col de la Biche and had to abandon the race with a fractured collarbone. On stage 12 to [Peyragudes](/wiki/Peyragudes "Peyragudes"), Froome cracked within the final kilometre, ceding 22 seconds and the yellow jersey to Aru. On the final approach on stage 14 to [Rodez](/wiki/Rodez "Rodez") the team stretched out the peloton, splitting it into several groups due to crosswinds allowing Froome to put 25 seconds into Aru, enough to retake yellow by a margin of 18 seconds overall.

On stage 15 to [Le Puy-en-Velay](/wiki/Le_Puy-en-Velay "Le Puy-en-Velay"), Froome ran into mechanical trouble on the ascent of the Col de Peyra Taillade and was distanced by more than 40 seconds thanks to a brutal acceleration by the AG2R La Mondiale team. With the help of his teammates he managed to make it back to the main group of GC contenders to keep his overall lead intact. Froome and Landa performed strongly in the Alpine stages to [Serre Chevalier](/wiki/Serre_Chevalier "Serre Chevalier") and [Izoard](/wiki/Col_d%27Izoard "Col d'Izoard"), helping Landa move into fourth overall.

In the final time trial in [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille"), Froome's strength in the discipline helped him put nearly two minutes into Romain Bardet and 25 seconds into Rigoberto Urán, finishing third on the stage six seconds behind stage winner [Maciej Bodnar](/wiki/Maciej_Bodnar "Maciej Bodnar") and 5 seconds behind second-place Kwiatkowski. Froome secured his fourth Tour victory and third consecutively, putting him one win away from joining the likes of Anquétil, Indurain, Merckx and Hinault. He became the 7th man to win the Tour overall without winning a stage, however he did amass ten top-ten finishes. Sky won the team classification for the first time in their history, leading from start to finish.

A week after the end of the Tour de France, Kwiatkowski continued his strong rides in the one-day classics by winning the [Clásica de San Sebastián](/wiki/2017_Cl%C3%A1sica_de_San_Sebasti%C3%A1n "2017 Clásica de San Sebastián"), and on 8 August signed a three-year contract extension.[[204]](#cite_note-204) After months of recovery following his injury, [Wout Poels](/wiki/Wout_Poels "Wout Poels") returned to form by winning the final stage of the [Tour de Pologne](/wiki/2017_Tour_de_Pologne "2017 Tour de Pologne") and finishing third overall, with teammate [Diego Rosa](/wiki/Diego_Rosa_(cyclist) "Diego Rosa (cyclist)") taking the mountains classification.[[205]](#cite_note-205) Landa followed up his performance in the Tour with overall victory in the [Vuelta a Burgos](/wiki/2017_Vuelta_a_Burgos "2017 Vuelta a Burgos"), also winning two stages, the mountains classification and the points classification.[[206]](#cite_note-206)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Podio_de_la_Vuelta_Ciclista_a_Espa%C3%B1a_2017.jpg/250px-Podio_de_la_Vuelta_Ciclista_a_Espa%C3%B1a_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Podio_de_la_Vuelta_Ciclista_a_Espa%C3%B1a_2017.jpg)

[Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome") (*centre*) on the podium after winning the [2017 Vuelta a España](/wiki/2017_Vuelta_a_Espa%C3%B1a "2017 Vuelta a España")

The team sent its strongest-ever squad to the [Vuelta a España](/wiki/2017_Vuelta_a_Espa%C3%B1a "2017 Vuelta a España"), with Froome on a mission to finally win the race that had eluded him on multiple occasions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] After a 4th-place finish in the opening team time trial in [Nîmes](/wiki/N%C3%AEmes "Nîmes"), Froome took third place on the third stage at [Andorra](/wiki/Andorra "Andorra") behind [Vincenzo Nibali](/wiki/Vincenzo_Nibali "Vincenzo Nibali"). Combined with bonus seconds picked up from the stage's intermediate sprint, it was enough to move him into the overall race lead, collecting his first red jersey since stage 10 in 2011. Several days later, he took his first stage win of his season at [Cumbre del Sol](/wiki/Benitachell "Benitachell"), which moved him into the lead in the points classification, followed by a second-place finish at [Calar Alto](/wiki/Calar_Alto "Calar Alto") on stage 11.

The following day to [Antequera](/wiki/Antequera "Antequera"), Froome crashed twice after a mechanical, but managed to limit his time losses with the help of his teammates and bounced back on the next 3 stages with consecutive top-10 finishes and regained the points lead after stage 15. Froome then dominated the stage 16 individual time trial to [Logroño](/wiki/Logro%C3%B1o "Logroño"), extending his lead to nearly 2 minutes ahead of Nibali. He struggled on the steep slopes to [Los Machucos](/wiki/Los_Machucos "Los Machucos") the next day, dropping about 40 seconds to Nibali. He regained half that loss on stage 18, and cemented the overall lead as well as the combination classification with a third-place finish at [Alto de l'Angliru](/wiki/Alto_de_l%27Angliru "Alto de l'Angliru") behind [Alberto Contador](/wiki/Alberto_Contador "Alberto Contador") on stage 20.

In a bunch sprint finish on the final stage in [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid"), he held on to the points classification by two points over [Matteo Trentin](/wiki/Matteo_Trentin "Matteo Trentin").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] With the victory, Froome became the first British rider to win the Vuelta, the third man to complete the Tour-Vuelta double in the same year (joining [Jacques Anquetil](/wiki/Jacques_Anquetil "Jacques Anquetil") and [Bernard Hinault](/wiki/Bernard_Hinault "Bernard Hinault")), and the first to complete the feat in the modern era since the Vuelta moved to its current August–September time period.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A week after the Vuelta victory, the team won the bronze medal in the team time trial at the [2017 UCI Road World Championships](/wiki/2017_UCI_Road_World_Championships "2017 UCI Road World Championships") in [Bergen](/wiki/Bergen "Bergen").[[207]](#cite_note-207)

On 30 June, the eve of the Tour de France Grand Départ, Froome signed a 2-year contract extension with the team.[[208]](#cite_note-208) The team announced the signings of [Jonathan Castroviejo](/wiki/Jonathan_Castroviejo "Jonathan Castroviejo")[[209]](#cite_note-209) ([Movistar Team](/wiki/Movistar_Team_(men%27s_team) "Movistar Team (men's team)")) and [David de la Cruz](/wiki/David_de_la_Cruz "David de la Cruz") ([Quick-Step Floors](/wiki/Soudal_Quick-Step "Soudal Quick-Step")) for the 2018 season.[[210]](#cite_note-210) On 27 August 2017, the team also announced the signing of 2017 [Tour de l'Avenir](/wiki/Tour_de_l%27Avenir "Tour de l'Avenir") winner [Egan Bernal](/wiki/Egan_Bernal "Egan Bernal") ([Androni–Sidermec–Bottecchia](/wiki/GW_Erco_Shimano "GW Erco Shimano")).[[211]](#cite_note-211) A day later, they announced the signings of reigning Under 23 Road Race World Champion [Kristoffer Halvorsen](/wiki/Kristoffer_Halvorsen "Kristoffer Halvorsen") ([Joker Icopal](/wiki/Joker_Fuel_of_Norway "Joker Fuel of Norway"))[[212]](#cite_note-212) and 2017 [Girobio](/wiki/Girobio "Girobio") overall winner [Pavel Sivakov](/wiki/Pavel_Sivakov "Pavel Sivakov") ([BMC Development Team](/wiki/BMC_Development_Team "BMC Development Team")),[[213]](#cite_note-213)

On 1 September, it was revealed that 2017 British Under-23 road race champion [Chris Lawless](/wiki/Chris_Lawless "Chris Lawless") had been signed from [Axeon–Hagens Berman](/wiki/Hagens_Berman_Jayco "Hagens Berman Jayco").[[214]](#cite_note-214) [Dylan van Baarle](/wiki/Dylan_van_Baarle "Dylan van Baarle") ([Cannondale–Drapac](/wiki/EF_Education%E2%80%93EasyPost "EF Education–EasyPost")) was signed on 18 September 2017.[[215]](#cite_note-215) On 5 December 2017, the team announced that they had signed [Leonardo Basso](/wiki/Leonardo_Basso "Leonardo Basso"), a stagaire with [Trek–Segafredo](/wiki/Lidl%E2%80%93Trek_(men%27s_team) "Lidl–Trek (men's team)").[[216]](#cite_note-216) Whilst [Mikel Landa](/wiki/Mikel_Landa "Mikel Landa") transferred to Movistar,[[217]](#cite_note-217) [Mikel Nieve](/wiki/Mikel_Nieve "Mikel Nieve") to [Mitchelton–Scott](/wiki/Team_Jayco%E2%80%93AlUla_(men%27s_team) "Team Jayco–AlUla (men's team)"),[[218]](#cite_note-218) [Peter Kennaugh](/wiki/Peter_Kennaugh "Peter Kennaugh") to [Bora–Hansgrohe](/wiki/Red_Bull%E2%80%93Bora%E2%80%93Hansgrohe "Red Bull–Bora–Hansgrohe")[[219]](#cite_note-219) and [Ian Boswell](/wiki/Ian_Boswell "Ian Boswell") to [Team Katusha–Alpecin](/wiki/Katusha%E2%80%93Alpecin "Katusha–Alpecin").[[220]](#cite_note-220) [Elia Viviani](/wiki/Elia_Viviani "Elia Viviani") ended his contract a year early to join QuickStep for the 2018 and 2019 seasons.[[221]](#cite_note-221) Christian Knees, Michał Gołaś, Sebastián Henao, Phil Deignan, David Lopez and Ian Stannard all signed multi-year contract extensions.[[222]](#cite_note-222)[[223]](#cite_note-223)[[224]](#cite_note-224)[[225]](#cite_note-225)[[226]](#cite_note-226)[[227]](#cite_note-227)

The team finished the 2017 season on top of the UCI World Tour team ranking for the first time since 2012. Chris Froome was the highest-ranked rider in the individual rankings, placing second.[[228]](#cite_note-228)

### 2018: Grand Tour domination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2018: Grand Tour domination")]

Main article: [2018 Team Sky season](/wiki/2018_Team_Sky_season "2018 Team Sky season")

The team's first victory of the year came via highly regarded new recruit, [Egan Bernal](/wiki/Egan_Bernal "Egan Bernal") claiming victory in the Colombian national time trial championships.[[229]](#cite_note-229) Teammate [Sergio Henao](/wiki/Sergio_Henao "Sergio Henao") would also claim victory in the national road race championships leading to a clean sweep of national titles.[[230]](#cite_note-230) Bernal continued his strong early season form, claiming overall victory at the [Colombia Oro y Paz](/wiki/Colombia_Oro_y_Paz "Colombia Oro y Paz").[[231]](#cite_note-231) As the European stage racing season kicked-off [Wout Poels](/wiki/Wout_Poels "Wout Poels") and [David de la Cruz](/wiki/David_de_la_Cruz "David de la Cruz") claimed victories at [Vuelta a Andalucía](/wiki/Vuelta_a_Andaluc%C3%ADa "Vuelta a Andalucía").[[232]](#cite_note-232)[[233]](#cite_note-233) [Michał Kwiatkowski](/wiki/Micha%C5%82_Kwiatkowski "Michał Kwiatkowski") and [Geraint Thomas](/wiki/Geraint_Thomas "Geraint Thomas") claimed a one–two victory at [Volta ao Algarve](/wiki/2018_Volta_ao_Algarve "2018 Volta ao Algarve").[[234]](#cite_note-234) Poels and de la Cruz continued their strong form, claiming stage victories at [Paris–Nice](/wiki/2018_Paris%E2%80%93Nice "2018 Paris–Nice"), whilst Kwiatkowski claimed a second overall general classification victory, winning [Tirreno–Adriatico](/wiki/2018_Tirreno%E2%80%93Adriatico "2018 Tirreno–Adriatico") – the team's first victory at the race.[[235]](#cite_note-235)

In the lead up to the first Grand Tour of the year, the [2018 Giro d'Italia](/wiki/2018_Giro_d%27Italia "2018 Giro d'Italia"), [Diego Rosa](/wiki/Diego_Rosa_(cyclist) "Diego Rosa (cyclist)") emerged victorious at [Settimana Internazionale di Coppi e Bartali](/wiki/Settimana_Internazionale_di_Coppi_e_Bartali "Settimana Internazionale di Coppi e Bartali"), claiming his first overall win for the team. New recruits [Chris Lawless](/wiki/Chris_Lawless "Chris Lawless") and [Pavel Sivakov](/wiki/Pavel_Sivakov "Pavel Sivakov") claimed their first classification victories for the team, with Lawless also winning a stage of the race. Bernal had been en route to claim second place at his first World Tour event of the year, [Volta a Catalunya](/wiki/2018_Volta_a_Catalunya "2018 Volta a Catalunya"), however a late crash on the final stage of the race saw Bernal abandon.[[236]](#cite_note-236) Bernal next raced at the [Tour de Romandie](/wiki/2018_Tour_de_Romandie "2018 Tour de Romandie") where he won the stage three time trial and with it, the young rider classification. Moving on to the [2018 Tour of California](/wiki/2018_Tour_of_California "2018 Tour of California"), Bernal lead the team's general classification ambitions, resulting in his first World Tour stage race victory, claiming two stages as well.[[237]](#cite_note-237)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/GIR40227_froome_poels.jpg/250px-GIR40227_froome_poels.jpg)](/wiki/File:GIR40227_froome_poels.jpg)

[Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome") (*right*) shortly before his decisive attack during the [2018 Giro d'Italia](/wiki/2018_Giro_d%27Italia "2018 Giro d'Italia")

[Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome") entered the Giro d'Italia as favourite to win the overall race. As the current champion of both the [Tour de France](/wiki/Tour_de_France "Tour de France") and [Vuelta a España](/wiki/Vuelta_a_Espa%C3%B1a "Vuelta a España"), Froome had the opportunity to join an elite group of riders who concurrently held all Grand Tour titles. The Giro started in [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") for the first time. Before the race began, Froome crashed during a recon of the first stage time trial route. Froome ceded over 30 seconds to main race rival, [Tom Dumoulin](/wiki/Tom_Dumoulin "Tom Dumoulin") as well as 20 seconds to [Simon Yates](/wiki/Simon_Yates_(cyclist) "Simon Yates (cyclist)"). As the race returned to Italian land, Froome found himself over 50 seconds down on the *maglia rosa* – [Rohan Dennis](/wiki/Rohan_Dennis "Rohan Dennis"). By the end of stage six, the races first summit finish at the top of [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna") Froome had moved up to eighth position overall, but had lost over a minute to new race leader, Yates.[[238]](#cite_note-238)

By the end of the eighth stage and third mountain-top finish at [Gran Sasso d'Italia](/wiki/Gran_Sasso_d%27Italia "Gran Sasso d'Italia"), Froome trailed Yates by nearly two and a half minutes, with general classification hopes looking all but impossible.[[239]](#cite_note-239) Froome's domestiques, Poels and Sergio Henao, were 18th and 25th overall 3' 14" and 5' 56" behind the race leader. By the end of stage 13, Froome had slipped back to 12th overall 3' 20" behind race leader, Yates.[[240]](#cite_note-240) Stage 14 saw the riders face the fearsome climb of [Monte Zoncolan](/wiki/Monte_Zoncolan "Monte Zoncolan"), with gradients reaching nearly 20%. Froome attacked the main selection of favourites, winning the stage from Yates.[[241]](#cite_note-241)

Froome's improved form was not maintained into stage 15, with Yates claiming the victory on the medium-mountain stage by a margin of 41 seconds over everyone else. Yates' lead in the race grew to 2' 11" over Dumoulin and 4' 52" over Froome – who was now sat in seventh place overall.[[242]](#cite_note-242) The time trial on stage 16, from [Trento](/wiki/Trento "Trento") to [Rovereto](/wiki/Rovereto "Rovereto"), had been a focus-point for Froome, hoping to claw back significant amounts of time over Yates' by virtue of his stronger time-trialling ability. Froome cut the deficit to 3' 50" by the end of the stage, having taken back over a minute from Yates.[[243]](#cite_note-243)

Deep into the third week of the race, stage 18 saw the first sign of weakness from Yates, cracking on the final climb of the day to [Prato Nevoso](/wiki/Prato_Nevoso "Prato Nevoso"), with the deficit to Dumoulin in second cut to 28 seconds and 3' 22" to Froome.[[244]](#cite_note-244) Stage 19 of the 2018-edition of the race had been designated as the races' [queen stage](/wiki/Queen_stage "Queen stage").
