Starting in [Venaria Reale](/wiki/Venaria_Reale "Venaria Reale"), the stage climbed over a trio of mountains; the part-gravel [Colle delle Finestre](/wiki/Colle_delle_Finestre "Colle delle Finestre"), with the climb to [Sestriere](/wiki/Sestriere "Sestriere") ending at [Bardonecchia](/wiki/Bardonecchia "Bardonecchia"). Yates' poor form continued into stage 19, culminating in him being dropped by the peloton on the lower slopes of the Finestre, promoting Domoulin to virtual race leader.[[245]](#cite_note-Cary-245)

With over 80 kilometres remaining in the stage Froome attacked the peloton on the gravel section of the Finestre, pulling away from the main group of favourites. This attack continued over the subsequent two climbs with Froome ultimately winning the stage by three minutes from second place [Richard Carapaz](/wiki/Richard_Carapaz "Richard Carapaz"), but importantly by over 3' 20" from Dumoulin. Froome was now leading the race overall by 40 seconds from Dumoulin with two stages remaining.[[245]](#cite_note-Cary-245) Froome maintained his race lead, becoming the fifth rider to simultaneously hold all three grand tour titles.[[246]](#cite_note-246)[[247]](#cite_note-247) Froome won the mountains classification largely due to his stage 19 exploits, and Team Sky won the overall team classification.

As the cycling season entered the European summer, the build to the [2018 Tour de France](/wiki/2018_Tour_de_France "2018 Tour de France") had begun. [Geraint Thomas](/wiki/Geraint_Thomas "Geraint Thomas") claimed overall victory at the [Critérium du Dauphiné](/wiki/2018_Crit%C3%A9rium_du_Dauphin%C3%A9 "2018 Critérium du Dauphiné") improving his chances of being promoted to team-leader for the Tour de France.[[248]](#cite_note-248) The team scored a number of national title victories: [Jonathan Castroviejo](/wiki/Jonathan_Castroviejo "Jonathan Castroviejo") won the Spanish time trial title, Kwiatkowski the Polish road race, [Dylan van Baarle](/wiki/Dylan_van_Baarle "Dylan van Baarle") the Dutch time trial, Thomas the British time trial and [Vasil Kiryienka](/wiki/Vasil_Kiryienka "Vasil Kiryienka") the Belarusian time trial.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/TDF34752_thomas_froome_%2841961100070%29.jpg/250px-TDF34752_thomas_froome_%2841961100070%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:TDF34752_thomas_froome_(41961100070).jpg)

[Geraint Thomas](/wiki/Geraint_Thomas "Geraint Thomas") (*left*) leads [Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome") during the [2018 Tour de France](/wiki/2018_Tour_de_France "2018 Tour de France").

As the Tour de France began, the team lead with a two-pronged attack, incumbent champion Froome hoping for a fourth straight grand tour win and fifth overall title and Thomas reigning Dauphiné champion and super-domestique in previous editions of the race. By the end of the first week of racing, Thomas had ridden a flawless race and was second overall, only six seconds behind [Greg Van Avermaet](/wiki/Greg_Van_Avermaet "Greg Van Avermaet"). Froome was 14th, over a minute behind the Belgian race leader.[[249]](#cite_note-249) Thomas and Froome both lost time to Van Avermaet on stage nine as the race transitioned across the cobbles of Roubaix, with Thomas 43 seconds and Froome 1' 42" behind the race leader. By the end of stage 10 these deficits had grown to 2' 22 for Thomas and 3' 21" for Froome.[[250]](#cite_note-250)

As the race entered the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") Thomas cemented his control on the race and the team, claiming consecutive victories at the summit finishes of [La Rosière](/wiki/La_Rosi%C3%A8re,_Savoie "La Rosière, Savoie") and [Alpe d'Huez](/wiki/Alpe_d%27Huez "Alpe d'Huez"). By the end of stage 12 Thomas lead had grown to 1' 39" over team-mate Froome.[[251]](#cite_note-251)[[252]](#cite_note-252) Colombian prodigy, Bernal, in his first grand tour of his career was 19th overall 21' 22" behind Thomas.[[253]](#cite_note-253) Following the 15th stage, controversy arouse around [Gianni Moscon](/wiki/Gianni_Moscon "Gianni Moscon"), who had been seen punching [Fortuneo–Samsic](/wiki/Ark%C3%A9a%E2%80%93Samsic "Arkéa–Samsic") rider [Élie Gesbert](/wiki/%C3%89lie_Gesbert "Élie Gesbert") during the opening kilometres of the stage. After reviewing footage of the incident, the race jury disqualified him for 'particularly serious aggression', leaving Sky with 7 riders for the remaining stages.[[254]](#cite_note-254)[[255]](#cite_note-255)

On stage 17 Froome cracked, slipping to third overall 2' 31" behind Thomas. This promoted Giro d'Italia runner-up, Tom Dumoulin, to second overall 1' 59" behind Welshman Thomas.[[256]](#cite_note-256) Thomas ultimately won the race overall, and the team recorded their fourth consecutive grand tour win, fifth Tour de France title and second successive year achieving more than one grand tour win.[[257]](#cite_note-257) Thanks to his second-place performance on the penultimate day time trial, Froome joined Thomas on the final podium finishing third overall.

Into the latter part of the season, Kwiatkowski maintained his good form from the Tour de France, winning his home stage race – the [Tour de Pologne](/wiki/2018_Tour_de_Pologne "2018 Tour de Pologne"), claiming two stage wins.[[258]](#cite_note-258) He headed into the final grand tour of the year, the [Vuelta a España](/wiki/Vuelta_a_Espa%C3%B1a "Vuelta a España"), as the team's general classification leader and finished 43rd overall, nearly an hour and three quarters behind race winner, Simon Yates. The teams best finisher was de la Cruz, 15th overall and 28 minutes behind the race winner.[[259]](#cite_note-259)

The team went on to claim two stage wins at the [Tour of Britain](/wiki/2018_Tour_of_Britain "2018 Tour of Britain") courtesy of Poels and [Ian Stannard](/wiki/Ian_Stannard "Ian Stannard"). [Gianni Moscon](/wiki/Gianni_Moscon "Gianni Moscon") returned after a five-week suspension in good form as the road to the final monument of the year began, winning [Coppa Ugo Agostoni](/wiki/Coppa_Ugo_Agostoni "Coppa Ugo Agostoni")[[260]](#cite_note-260) and the [Giro della Toscana](/wiki/Giro_di_Toscana "Giro di Toscana"),[[261]](#cite_note-261) as well as the Italian national time trial title. Moscon went on to take the overall victory at the [2018 Tour of Guangxi](/wiki/2018_Tour_of_Guangxi "2018 Tour of Guangxi") – the team's final victory of the year.[[262]](#cite_note-262)

### 2019: Transition to new sponsorship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2019: Transition to new sponsorship")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Tour_de_France_2019%2C_Egan_Bernal_and_Geraint_Thomas_%2848416905411%29.jpg/250px-Tour_de_France_2019%2C_Egan_Bernal_and_Geraint_Thomas_%2848416905411%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tour_de_France_2019,_Egan_Bernal_and_Geraint_Thomas_(48416905411).jpg)

[Egan Bernal](/wiki/Egan_Bernal "Egan Bernal") (*left*) and [Geraint Thomas](/wiki/Geraint_Thomas "Geraint Thomas") at the [2019 Tour de France](/wiki/2019_Tour_de_France "2019 Tour de France")

Main article: [2019 Team Ineos season](/wiki/2019_Team_Ineos_season "2019 Team Ineos season")

In mid-December 2018, [Sky](/wiki/Sky_plc "Sky plc") announced they would withdraw their sponsorship as part of an ongoing review brought on by their acquisition by [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast"). [21st Century Fox](/wiki/21st_Century_Fox "21st Century Fox") also announced that it would end its partnership at the end of the season, forcing the team into a sponsorship search in order to continue.[[263]](#cite_note-263) In February 2019, *[Cyclingnews.com](/wiki/Cyclingnews.com "Cyclingnews.com")* reported that potential sponsorship could be sourced via a combination of the Colombian government and state-controlled oil and gas company, [Ecopetrol](/wiki/Ecopetrol "Ecopetrol"). Later reports claimed that no deal had been forthcoming.[[264]](#cite_note-264)

In mid-March, Cyclingnews.com again broke the news that another potential sponsor had been found. The report linked the team with British-based multi-national chemical company, [Ineos](/wiki/Ineos "Ineos"), with the team expected to become *Team Ineos* for the 2020 season. Ineos is controlled by Manchester-born billionaire, [Jim Ratcliffe](/wiki/Jim_Ratcliffe "Jim Ratcliffe"), who has amassed an estimated fortune of over £21 billion.[[265]](#cite_note-TT-265) Ratcliffe has also invested over £100 million in [Ben Ainslie](/wiki/Ben_Ainslie "Ben Ainslie")'s sailing team. The web-domain "TeamIneos.com" was discovered to have been registered on 5 March, and the [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") handle "@teamineos" was also registered.[[266]](#cite_note-266)

On 19 March, Team Sky confirmed its new title sponsor, announcing it was to be renamed 'Team Ineos' on 1 May, ahead of the 2019 Tour de Yorkshire.[[267]](#cite_note-auto-267) However, the UCI's rules prevent teams from racing under different names at the same time – Sky had planned to take part in the [2019 Tour de Romandie](/wiki/2019_Tour_de_Romandie "2019 Tour de Romandie"), starting on 30 April as well as the Tour de Yorkshire. As a consequence, it announced that the Romandie would be the first race under the Ineos name, with the team wearing a special black kit, while the Yorkshire would see the launch of the team's new colours.[[7]](#cite_note-change-7)

For the 2019 season the team signed world individual pursuit champion, [Filippo Ganna](/wiki/Filippo_Ganna "Filippo Ganna")[[268]](#cite_note-268) and former rider, [Ben Swift](/wiki/Ben_Swift "Ben Swift") ([UAE Team Emirates](/wiki/UAE_Team_Emirates_XRG "UAE Team Emirates XRG"));[[269]](#cite_note-269) former Ecuadorian national road champion, [Jhonatan Narváez](/wiki/Jhonatan_Narv%C3%A1ez "Jhonatan Narváez") ([Quick-Step Floors](/wiki/Soudal_Quick-Step "Soudal Quick-Step")).[[270]](#cite_note-270) After a protracted transfer the team also signed Colombian talent – [Iván Sosa](/wiki/Iv%C3%A1n_Sosa "Iván Sosa") ([Androni Giocattoli–Sidermec](/wiki/GW_Erco_Shimano "GW Erco Shimano")).[[271]](#cite_note-271) [Philip Deignan](/wiki/Philip_Deignan "Philip Deignan")[[272]](#cite_note-272) and [David López](/wiki/David_L%C3%B3pez_(cyclist) "David López (cyclist)") retired,[[273]](#cite_note-273) [Sergio Henao](/wiki/Sergio_Henao "Sergio Henao") left to join [UAE Team Emirates](/wiki/UAE_Team_Emirates_XRG "UAE Team Emirates XRG"),[[274]](#cite_note-274) [Beñat Intxausti](/wiki/Be%C3%B1at_Intxausti "Beñat Intxausti") joined [Euskadi–Murias](/wiki/Euskadi%E2%80%93Murias "Euskadi–Murias")[[275]](#cite_note-275) and [Łukasz Wiśniowski](/wiki/%C5%81ukasz_Wi%C5%9Bniowski "Łukasz Wiśniowski") joined the newly Polish-sponsored BMC team, [CCC Pro Team](/wiki/CCC_Pro_Team "CCC Pro Team").[[276]](#cite_note-276) [Jonathan Dibben](/wiki/Jonathan_Dibben "Jonathan Dibben") also left the team.

### 2020: Ineos Grenadiers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 2020: Ineos Grenadiers")]

For the 2020 season the team announced a number of new signings: [2019 Giro d'Italia](/wiki/2019_Giro_d%27Italia "2019 Giro d'Italia") champion, [Richard Carapaz](/wiki/Richard_Carapaz "Richard Carapaz") ([Movistar Team](/wiki/Movistar_Team_(men%27s_team) "Movistar Team (men's team)")), double and reigning [Time Trial World Champion](/wiki/2019_UCI_Road_World_Championships "2019 UCI Road World Championships"), [Rohan Dennis](/wiki/Rohan_Dennis "Rohan Dennis"), [Ethan Hayter](/wiki/Ethan_Hayter "Ethan Hayter"), [Brandon Rivera](/wiki/Brandon_Rivera "Brandon Rivera") ([GW–Shimano](/wiki/GW%E2%80%93Shimano "GW–Shimano")) and [Carlos Rodriguez](/wiki/Carlos_Rodriguez_(cyclist) "Carlos Rodriguez (cyclist)").[[277]](#cite_note-277)

[David de la Cruz](/wiki/David_de_la_Cruz "David de la Cruz") left the team to join [UAE Team Emirates](/wiki/UAE_Team_Emirates_XRG "UAE Team Emirates XRG"), [Kenny Elissonde](/wiki/Kenny_Elissonde "Kenny Elissonde") joined [Trek–Segafredo](/wiki/Lidl%E2%80%93Trek_(men%27s_team) "Lidl–Trek (men's team)"), [Kristoffer Halvorsen](/wiki/Kristoffer_Halvorsen "Kristoffer Halvorsen") joined ([EF Education First Pro Cycling](/wiki/EF_Education_First_Pro_Cycling "EF Education First Pro Cycling")), [Wout Poels](/wiki/Wout_Poels "Wout Poels") joined [Team Bahrain McLaren](/wiki/Team_Bahrain_McLaren "Team Bahrain McLaren") and [Diego Rosa](/wiki/Diego_Rosa_(cyclist) "Diego Rosa (cyclist)") joined [Arkéa–Samsic](/wiki/Ark%C3%A9a%E2%80%93Samsic "Arkéa–Samsic").[[278]](#cite_note-278)

Ineos had the highest payroll of any team in cycling for the 2020 season, and of the top ten highest paid riders in the sport, five of them rode for Ineos. In Euros, Carapaz was paid 2.1 million, Kwiatkowski 2.5 million, Bernal 2.7 million, Geraint Thomas 3.5 million and Chris Froome was the 2nd highest paid cyclist in the sport contracted to make 4.5 million.[[279]](#cite_note-279)

On 30 January 2020, the team announced the retirement of [Vasil Kiryienka](/wiki/Vasil_Kiryienka "Vasil Kiryienka") due to heart problems.[[280]](#cite_note-Kiryienka_retirement-280) The following day, Australian [Cameron Wurf](/wiki/Cameron_Wurf "Cameron Wurf") joined the squad.[[281]](#cite_note-Wurf_joins-281)

On 3 March 2020, the team's lead sports director [Nicolas Portal](/wiki/Nicolas_Portal "Nicolas Portal") died suddenly of a heart attack at the age of 40. A day later, the team announced that it would temporarily withdraw from racing until 23 March in time for the [Volta a Catalunya](/wiki/Volta_a_Catalunya "Volta a Catalunya"). The reasoning behind the decision was twofold: to allow the team to properly mourn Portal's passing, and to keep all its members safe amidst the growing coronavirus outbreak which saw the final two stages of the UAE Tour cancelled.[[282]](#cite_note-282)

Additional races on the 2020 calendar were either postponed to later in the year or cancelled outright due to restrictions and lockdowns in several European countries forced by the pandemic. On 12 April, the entire team took part in a special event via interactive cycling site [Zwift](/wiki/Zwift "Zwift"). Over 15,000 fans from around the world rode with the team in a virtual group ride, which was followed by an e-race where all 30 riders on the active roster competed against each other. It was streamed on YouTube and the team's Facebook page, with commentary by Eurosport's Rob Hatch and Matt Stephens. The race was won by [Rohan Dennis](/wiki/Rohan_Dennis "Rohan Dennis").[[283]](#cite_note-283)

On 9 July 2020, it was announced that the contract of [Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome") would not be renewed, ending his 10-year association with the team.[[284]](#cite_note-284) An hour later, Froome signed a multi-year deal with [Israel Start-Up Nation](/wiki/Israel_Start-Up_Nation "Israel Start-Up Nation") for the 2021 season.[[285]](#cite_note-285)

On 12 October 2022, the team announced the signing of their first ever female rider; the multi discipline star [Pauline Ferrand-Prévot](/wiki/Pauline_Ferrand-Pr%C3%A9vot "Pauline Ferrand-Prévot").[[286]](#cite_note-286)

## Sponsorship and budgets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Sponsorship and budgets")]

According to the results of a study commissioned by [Cyclingnews.com](/wiki/Cyclingnews.com "Cyclingnews.com") and performed by Repucom, the team gave more media value to their sponsors and partners than any other cycling team. The team delivered approximately $550m in advertising value, the highest amount achieved by any professional team.[[287]](#cite_note-teamsky-21st-287)

BSkyB provided £30 million in sponsorship for the team and will back the team as name sponsor until the end of 2013.[[4]](#cite_note-guardian.co.uk-4) The team also receives further sponsorship from [21st Century Fox](/wiki/21st_Century_Fox "21st Century Fox") (previously [News Corporation](/wiki/News_Corporation_(1980%E2%80%932013) "News Corporation (1980–2013)")) and [Sky Italia](/wiki/Sky_Italia "Sky Italia"). [Pinarello](/wiki/Pinarello "Pinarello") supplies bicycle frames and forks.[[288]](#cite_note-288)[[289]](#cite_note-289)[[290]](#cite_note-290) On 5 January 2010, [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas") were announced as the team's official apparel and accessories partner.[[291]](#cite_note-291)

[Gatorade](/wiki/Gatorade "Gatorade"), [Marks & Spencer](/wiki/Marks_%26_Spencer "Marks & Spencer"), [Oakley](/wiki/Oakley,_Inc. "Oakley, Inc."), [IG Markets](/wiki/IG_Group "IG Group") are additional sponsors. [Jaguar](/wiki/Jaguar_Cars "Jaguar Cars") are providers of the team cars.
The team jerseys were changed to black and green beginning with the [2011 Tour de France](/wiki/2011_Tour_de_France "2011 Tour de France"), when the team formed Sky Rainforest Rescue, a three-year partnership with [WWF](/wiki/World_Wide_Fund_for_Nature "World Wide Fund for Nature") to help raise awareness of [deforestation in Brazil](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Brazil "Deforestation in Brazil").[[292]](#cite_note-292) At the [2018 Tour de France](/wiki/2018_Tour_de_France "2018 Tour de France"), the team wore special kit in support of the Sky Ocean Rescue initiative, with the goal of eliminating single-use plastic team-wide by 2020.[[293]](#cite_note-293)

On 25 June 2013, the team announced that the logo of 21st Century Fox, the direct successor to News Corporation following the [spin-off of its publishing business](/wiki/News_Corp_(2013%E2%80%93present) "News Corp (2013–present)"), would appear on the team's kit and team vehicles.[[287]](#cite_note-teamsky-21st-287)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Tour_de_France_2012_Saint-R%C3%A9my-l%C3%A8s-Chevreuse_088.jpg/250px-Tour_de_France_2012_Saint-R%C3%A9my-l%C3%A8s-Chevreuse_088.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tour_de_France_2012_Saint-R%C3%A9my-l%C3%A8s-Chevreuse_088.jpg)

The team support car at the [2012 Tour de France](/wiki/2012_Tour_de_France "2012 Tour de France"), with a change of [livery](/wiki/Livery "Livery") due to rider [Bradley Wiggins](/wiki/Bradley_Wiggins "Bradley Wiggins") wearing the [yellow jersey](/wiki/Yellow_jersey "Yellow jersey").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/2019_ToB_stage_1_-_Team_Ineos.JPG/250px-2019_ToB_stage_1_-_Team_Ineos.JPG)](/wiki/File:2019_ToB_stage_1_-_Team_Ineos.JPG)

Team Ineos riders in the [Castelli](/wiki/Castelli_(brand) "Castelli (brand)") jerseys of their new sponsor at the [2019 Tour of Britain](/wiki/2019_Tour_of_Britain "2019 Tour of Britain")

On the second rest day of the [2016 Tour de France](/wiki/2016_Tour_de_France "2016 Tour de France"), the team announced they had signed a four-year extension with Pinarello – supplying the team with bikes until 2020.[[294]](#cite_note-294)

[Castelli](/wiki/Castelli_(brand) "Castelli (brand)") started providing the team's kit beginning in 2017 after their 3-year partnership with [Rapha](/wiki/Rapha_(sportswear) "Rapha (sportswear)") ended. The new kit was revealed during the Rouleur Classic event on 3 November 2016.[[295]](#cite_note-295)

On 12 December 2018, Sky's parent company [21st Century Fox](/wiki/21st_Century_Fox "21st Century Fox") confirmed that they would withdraw sponsorship of the team at the end of the 2019 season, but would until the [2019 Tour de France](/wiki/2019_Tour_de_France "2019 Tour de France") seek a new sponsor.[[296]](#cite_note-296)[[297]](#cite_note-297)

On 19 March 2019, Team Sky announced that [Ineos](/wiki/Ineos "Ineos"), a multinational chemicals company owned by [Jim Ratcliffe](/wiki/Jim_Ratcliffe "Jim Ratcliffe"), would become the new title sponsor as of 1 May 2019.[[267]](#cite_note-auto-267)

In 2021, the team announced that Belgian kit manufacturer Bioracer will provide the team kit from 2022 onwards.[[298]](#cite_note-298)[[299]](#cite_note-299)

On 13 December 2023, the team announced that Gobik would become their new on-bike race apparal partner from 2024, replacing Bioracer as the manufacturer of the teams kits in a 'long-term partnership.'[[300]](#cite_note-300)[[301]](#cite_note-301)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Media")]

A five-part documentary series following the team's 2012 season, *Team Sky and British Cycling: The Road to Glory*, premiered on [Sky Atlantic](/wiki/Sky_Atlantic "Sky Atlantic") on 30 August 2012.[[310]](#cite_note-310) Another documentary *Bradley Wiggins: A Year in Yellow*, following Wiggins's exploits in the 2012 season was first shown on the same channel in November 2012.[[311]](#cite_note-311) The team have also produced two books chronicling the 2012 Tour de France and 2013 season- *21 Days to Glory*[[312]](#cite_note-312) and *The Pain and the Glory*.

## Doping policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Doping policy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/David_Brailsford.jpg/250px-David_Brailsford.jpg)](/wiki/File:David_Brailsford.jpg)

The team's general manager, [David Brailsford](/wiki/David_Brailsford "David Brailsford")

The team claims to have a [zero-tolerance](/wiki/Zero-tolerance "Zero-tolerance") approach to [doping](/wiki/Doping_in_cycling "Doping in cycling").[[313]](#cite_note-313)[[314]](#cite_note-Doping-scrutiny-314) All its riders and staff must sign an agreement that they have no past or present involvement in taking illegal substances. Anyone breaching the agreement at any time must leave the squad.[[315]](#cite_note-DopingPolicy-315) Previous team members such as team doctor Geert Leinders,[[316]](#cite_note-Tainted_History-316) sports director Steven de Jongh and coach Bobby Julich[[317]](#cite_note-StaffDeparted-317) have all left the team when their involvement in doping prior to working with Sky became known.

This approach has been criticised by David Howman of [WADA](/wiki/World_Anti-Doping_Agency "World Anti-Doping Agency"), who has argued that fear of losing their job will discourage people with a history of doping from confessing.[[318]](#cite_note-Doping-318)

Although there have been speculations that Team Sky's tactics and success imply use of banned substances, Brailsford has strenuously denied any team use of illegal substances, citing his team's success in the Olympics as proof that you don't need to dope to dominate.[[319]](#cite_note-Performance-enhancing-319)

In September 2013, [Jonathan Tiernan-Locke](/wiki/Jonathan_Tiernan-Locke "Jonathan Tiernan-Locke") was asked by the UCI to explain a potential discrepancy in his [biological passport](/wiki/Biological_passport "Biological passport") data.[[320]](#cite_note-320) In December 2013, British Cycling confirmed it had been instructed to begin disciplinary proceedings against Tiernan-Locke by the UCI.[[321]](#cite_note-Suspension-321) Sky stated the blood values in question were taken in 2012, when Tiernan-Locke was a member of the [Endura Racing](/wiki/Endura_Racing "Endura Racing") squad, and he was suspended from all team activities pending a decision.[[321]](#cite_note-Suspension-321) In July 2014 Tiernan-Locke was banned from competition until 31 December 2015 by the UCI, resulting in his contract with the team being terminated with immediate effect.[[322]](#cite_note-322)[[323]](#cite_note-323)

In March 2014, [Sergio Henao](/wiki/Sergio_Henao "Sergio Henao") was removed from race schedules for at least eight weeks pending the conclusion of an "altitude research programme", following tests that were taken over the winter whilst Henao was training at altitude in Colombia.[[324]](#cite_note-324) In June 2014, Henao returned to racing at the [Tour de Suisse](/wiki/2014_Tour_de_Suisse "2014 Tour de Suisse"), after completing an independent research programme investigating the physiology of "altitude natives" in conjunction with the [University of Sheffield](/wiki/University_of_Sheffield "University of Sheffield").[[325]](#cite_note-325)

In April 2016, the Cycling Anti-Doping Foundation (CADF) opened an investigation into Sergio Henao's biological passport data from between 2011 and 2015.[[326]](#cite_note-326) As a result, the team withdrew Henao from their active roster.[[327]](#cite_note-327) After investigation, including a review of the research done in the previous year for Team Sky, CADF declared Henao had no case to answer, and he was restored to the racing squad.

In December 2017 [Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome") returned an adverse analytical finding (AAF) for salbutamol over the WADA threshold of 1000 ng/mL, potentially resulting in a ban and the loss of his 2017 Vuelta title.[[328]](#cite_note-328) On 2 July 2018, the UCI with the assistance of WADA concluded there was no AAF upon further investigation of the evidence and closed the case against Froome, exonerating him from any wrongdoing which allowed his Vuelta title to stand. Both Froome and the team welcomed the decision.[[329]](#cite_note-329)[[330]](#cite_note-330)

### TUEs, UKAD and subsequent fallout

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: TUEs, UKAD and subsequent fallout")]

#### Therapeutic use exemptions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Therapeutic use exemptions")]

In September 2016, Russian [cyber espionage](/wiki/Cyber_espionage "Cyber espionage") group [Fancy Bear](/wiki/Fancy_Bear "Fancy Bear")[[331]](#cite_note-331) hacked the [World Anti-Doping Agency](/wiki/World_Anti-Doping_Agency "World Anti-Doping Agency") (WADA) ADAMS anti-doping system and released data on a number of athletes, including [Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome") and [Bradley Wiggins](/wiki/Bradley_Wiggins "Bradley Wiggins"). Therapeutic use exemptions (TUEs) are medical exemptions athletes can be given when they need treatment for pre-existing medical conditions where the drugs used in the treatment are on the WADA prohibited compound list.[[332]](#cite_note-cyclingnews.com-332)

The leak demonstrated that Wiggins had received TUEs for [triamcinolone acetonide](/wiki/Triamcinolone_acetonide "Triamcinolone acetonide") in June 2011, June 2012 and April 2013, a number of days before the start of the [2011 Tour de France](/wiki/2011_Tour_de_France "2011 Tour de France"), [2012 Tour de France](/wiki/2012_Tour_de_France "2012 Tour de France"), and [2013 Giro d'Italia](/wiki/2013_Giro_d%27Italia "2013 Giro d'Italia") respectively.[[332]](#cite_note-cyclingnews.com-332) Wiggins also received TUEs for [salbutamol](/wiki/Salbutamol "Salbutamol"), [fluticasone](/wiki/Fluticasone "Fluticasone"), [formoterol](/wiki/Formoterol "Formoterol") and [budesonide](/wiki/Budesonide "Budesonide") whilst at [Team Highroad](/wiki/HTC%E2%80%93Highroad "HTC–Highroad").[[333]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-333) Froome had received TUEs for [prednisolone](/wiki/Prednisolone "Prednisolone") in May 2013 and during the [2014 Tour de Romandie](/wiki/2014_Tour_de_Romandie "2014 Tour de Romandie").[[334]](#cite_note-334)

In response Froome said he had "no issues" with the leak whilst Wiggins's spokesperson said "there's nothing new here".[[332]](#cite_note-cyclingnews.com-332)[[333]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-333)

#### UKAD investigation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: UKAD investigation")]

In October 2016, [UK Anti-Doping](/wiki/UK_Anti-Doping "UK Anti-Doping") (UKAD) opened an investigation into Team Sky and [British Cycling](/wiki/British_Cycling "British Cycling"). It was revealed that [Simon Cope](/wiki/Simon_Cope "Simon Cope") delivered a package, to the team, during the [2011 Critérium du Dauphiné](/wiki/2011_Crit%C3%A9rium_du_Dauphin%C3%A9 "2011 Critérium du Dauphiné"), giving it to team Doctor [Richard Freeman](/wiki/Richard_Freeman_(physician) "Richard Freeman (physician)") confirming the package contained some form of medicine.[[335]](#cite_note-335) Neither the team nor Cope could confirm the exact contents of the package.[[336]](#cite_note-336) Former rider, Jonathan Tiernan-Locke (who has since served a ban for an anti-doping violation) claimed that Freeman had administered [Tramadol](/wiki/Tramadol "Tramadol") to the British national team during the [2012 UCI Road World Championships](/wiki/2012_UCI_Road_World_Championships_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_road_race "2012 UCI Road World Championships – Men's road race").[[337]](#cite_note-337)

In late October, [parliament](/wiki/UK_Government "UK Government") announced it would open an investigation into the relationship between the medical package and former rider, Bradley Wiggins.[[338]](#cite_note-338) In December 2016, UCI President [Brian Cookson](/wiki/Brian_Cookson "Brian Cookson") urged both Team Sky and general manager, [Dave Brailsford](/wiki/Dave_Brailsford "Dave Brailsford") to give "full disclosure" about what was in the package.[[339]](#cite_note-339) British Cycling has warned Cope about his relationship with both the team as well as the British National team.[[340]](#cite_note-340)

In December 2016, Brailsford announced that the package contained the legal drug, [Fluimucil](/wiki/Fluimucil "Fluimucil") – a [mucolytic](/wiki/Mucolytic "Mucolytic") compound which helps the body to remove sticky and thick mucus that can often be found obstructing the airway, resulting in coughing.[[341]](#cite_note-341) British Cycling officials maintained that they did not know the contents of the package.[[342]](#cite_note-342)

In March 2017, British Cycling admitted its failure in correctly recording the contents of the package,[[343]](#cite_note-343) whilst the team maintained that no anti-doping rules had been broken.[[344]](#cite_note-344)

In early March news broke around a number of riders potentially considering asking Brailsford to resign from the team,[[345]](#cite_note-345) however on the same day [Geraint Thomas](/wiki/Geraint_Thomas "Geraint Thomas"), [Luke Rowe](/wiki/Luke_Rowe "Luke Rowe"), [Tao Geoghegan Hart](/wiki/Tao_Geoghegan_Hart "Tao Geoghegan Hart"), [Peter Kennaugh](/wiki/Peter_Kennaugh "Peter Kennaugh"), [Michał Kwiatkowski](/wiki/Micha%C5%82_Kwiatkowski "Michał Kwiatkowski") and [Elia Viviani](/wiki/Elia_Viviani "Elia Viviani") came out in support of their General Manager.[[346]](#cite_note-346)

In March, former team rider, [Joshua Edmondson](/wiki/Josh_Edmondson_(cyclist) "Josh Edmondson (cyclist)") admitted to the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") that he violated the team's "no needle" policy by injecting himself with a cocktail of vitamins – [carnitine](/wiki/Carnitine "Carnitine"), [folic acid](/wiki/Folic_acid "Folic acid"), 'TAD' (reduced [glutathione](/wiki/Glutathione "Glutathione")[[347]](#cite_note-347)) – two or three times a week, for a month. The team stated that they found the vitamins, which were not prohibited compounds, and needles in the riders room.[[348]](#cite_note-348) A day later the UCI's Cycling Anti-Doping Foundation asked UK Anti-Doping to assess Edmondson's admission.[[349]](#cite_note-349)

On 15 November 2017, the UKAD announced that it had closed its investigation and filed no charges, citing that it was impossible to determine the contents of the package. Both the team and British Cycling issued statements welcoming its conclusion.[[350]](#cite_note-350)[[351]](#cite_note-351)

### UK Parliamentary report into "Combatting doping in sport"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: UK Parliamentary report into \"Combatting doping in sport\"")]

In March 2018, [The Commons Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Committee](/wiki/The_Commons_Digital,_Culture,_Media_and_Sport_Committee "The Commons Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Committee") published the report *Combatting doping in sport*. The report stated that Team Sky had "crossed an ethical line" by using medical drugs to "enhance the performance of riders" and that Brailsford must "take responsibility for the "damaging scepticism about the legitimacy of his team's performance and accomplishments."[[352]](#cite_note-352)[[353]](#cite_note-353)

Following the report, Bradley Wiggins, in an interview with the BBC, claimed that he "100 per cent did not cheat", and believed he was a victim of a smear campaign. He was also critical of the 'anonymous' source, demanding that the source should be made public.[[354]](#cite_note-cyclingnews.com1-354) Chris Froome later came out in support of Brailsford remaining team principal, rubbishing the accusations laid out in the report[[355]](#cite_note-355) whilst Geraint Thomas said that the team had never even joked about using corticosteroids.[[356]](#cite_note-356)

Former rider, [Bernhard Eisel](/wiki/Bernhard_Eisel "Bernhard Eisel") being interviewed at the 2018 [Tirreno–Adriatico](/wiki/Tirreno%E2%80%93Adriatico "Tirreno–Adriatico"), also denied any drug use during his tenure with the team.[[357]](#cite_note-357) Eisel also criticised reports for a lack of verifiable proof, further suggesting that this had "created a vacuum, within which he [Eisel] felt social media had filled in the blanks" as well as criticising comments made by former cyclist, [Floyd Landis](/wiki/Floyd_Landis "Floyd Landis"), regarding the removal of Wiggins's 2012 title.[[358]](#cite_note-358)

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ineos_Grenadiers&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Awards")]

* *[Velonews.com](/wiki/Competitor_Group,_Inc. "Competitor Group, Inc.") Velo Awards – Best Men's Team* (2013)[[360]](#cite_note-competitor.com-360)
* *[Velonews.com](/wiki/Competitor_Group,_Inc. "Competitor Group, Inc.") Velo Awards – Support Rider of the Year* – Richie Porte (2013)[[360]](#cite_note-competitor.com-360)
* *[Vélo d'Or](/wiki/V%C3%A9lo_d%27Or "Vélo d'Or") – Best rider* – [Chris Froome](/wiki/Chris_Froome "Chris Froome") (2013, 2015, 2017)[[361]](#cite_note-361)
* *London Design Awards – Product Design, Sport and Active life category* – for the Dogma F8 in conjunction with [Pinarello](/wiki/Pinarello "Pinarello") and [Jaguar](/wiki/Jaguar_Cars "Jaguar Cars") (2014)[[362]](#cite_note-362)[[363]](#cite_note-363)[[364]](#cite_note-364)
* *European Sponsorship Association Excellence Awards – Best Use of PR* – in conjunction with [Jaguar](/wiki/Jaguar_Cars "Jaguar Cars") (2014)[[365]](#cite_note-365)[[366]](#cite_note-366)
* *[Cyclingnews.com](/wiki/Cyclingnews.com "Cyclingnews.com") – Best Male Team* (2015, 2016, 2017)[[367]](#cite_note-367)[[368]](#cite_note-368)[[369]](#cite_note-369)
* *Cyclingtps.com.au CT Awards – Ultimate Team Player* – Richie Porte (2015)[[370]](#cite_note-cyclingtips.com.au-370)
* *Cyclingtps.com.au CT Awards – Most Impressive Team* (2015)[[370]](#cite_note-cyclingtips.com.au-370)
* *[Cyclingnews.com](/wiki/Cyclingnews.com "Cyclingnews.com") – Moment of the Year* (Chris Froome's run up Mont Ventoux, 2016)[[371]](#cite_note-371)
* *[Cyclingnews.com](/wiki/Cyclingnews.com "Cyclingnews.com") – Best Male Road Rider* – Chris Froome (2017)[[372]](#cite_note-372)
* *[Cyclingnews.com](/wiki/Cyclingnews.com "Cyclingnews.com") – Rider of the Year* – Chris Froome (2017)[[373]](#cite_note-373)
* *Sports Journalists' Association Sportsman of the Year* – Chris Froome (2017)[[374]](#cite_note-374)
* *Liontrust's Sporting Hero award* – Chris Froome (September 2017)[[375]](#cite_note-375)
